In the last 10 years, 1,600 laws, old antiquated statutes have been removed from the statute book. Can you
give a similar example anywhere in the world? 1,600 laws have been removed. 35,000 compliances have
been removed. big exercise is right now going to reshape the legal structure tuned to modern times
because many of the laws were written in a time when the government and the uh political setup of that
period I'm saying of the early from 1950 to 1990 that was a period when the government governments
were basically very inwardlooking they were very protectionist they were not looking at the energy of our
people they were trying to control that energy so all many of those laws are being rewritten now so that's a
major exercise which is going on second many of the procedural things are getting changed Mr. ML has
witnessed to get a telecom tower permit it used to take 270 days now it takes 7 days to get a railway
terminal connected to the railway network it used to take 6 years now it takes just about 2 and 1/2 months
so that is the kind of simplification procedural as well as legal is happening in the country our entire criminal
justice system which was of 1800 uh some vintage so that has now totally been transformed into a new
structure The telecom act was of 1854 vintage that has been replaced by a brand new 2023 act. So
practically every sector we are trying to bring in a totally new legal framework or totally new procedural
framework built on the technology stack that we have.

[music] Thanks a lot and uh it's a great honor for me to be at this stage and we just heard the talk by Manish

AI, what we plan to do when we host the AI impact summit in February 2026 and why India should be at the
radar of everyone thinking of AI, everyone thinking of technology, everyone thinking of healthcare. So this is
uh some progress that we have seen in the last few years. We have a platform called E Sanjienney, a tele
medicine platform which was doing almost 5,000 doctor consultations way back in 2020 January. And when
COVID came in, there's a need to scale up this platform to make it into a OPD platform wherein people
could consult doctors without having to go to a physical hospital. We scaled up that platform and today in
the last 5 years we have served more than 309 million patients through that platform. The daily number of
consultations that we are doing is in excess of 500,000. We have has significant uh progress that we have
made in various health parameters like for example life expectancy has gone up, death rate has reduced
greatly. We have number of doctors who are running it though the doctor to population people ratio is
much lower compared to what's uh elsewhere but still we have a huge workforce and our doctor doctors
are not only serving patients in India but many of them actually the best of them do go out and serve the
western countries as well. So we do have talent when it comes to medicine. We have almost 172,000 what
we call it health clinics across the India. We call them Aayushman Aro Mandr where people can go for
health and wellness checks and more importantly we have more than 10,000 plus startups who are working
in this field. Some of them are here today in fact I can see Vikalp of Ekkair who built the Aroetu app when
we were battling COVID 19 and there are many more like him about which we'll be talking in a in a little
while. So the so what what what can AI do in healthcare? Of course we we saw what Manish presented
about the various applications that AI can do. So what we look at it is that it can enable access. It can enable
quality healthcare. It can reduce cost of access for quality health conditions. In access what we are looking
at screening we are looking at remote personalized uh recommendations. We are looking at how drug
discovery can be enhanced by using artificial intelligence quality like we are uh very often there are several
cases in India several diseases that go undiagnosed because we are not able to provide specialist doctors in
remote areas like for example tuberculosis remains to be a big killer in India and very often in remote areas
we do have hospitals we do have x-ray machines but we don't have quality radiologists so we have AI
enabled solutions which can solve this problem which can look read the X-ray images and give diagnosis
that is required. public health surveillance, monitoring epidemics, monitoring diseases, deni is a big uh deni,
malaria, these are like common problems that we face. But collecting data from remote locations through
projects like Aayushman Bharat digital mission, we are able to analyze, we are able to predict where the
hotspots, how we can how we can take necessary actions to prevent spread of an epidemic. Cost has come
down. In fact, the cost of accessing quality health care by use of technology, by use of AI enabled services
has really come down and India is able to provide quality health care through schemes like Aishwan Bhbad
health insurance which guarantees uh insurance coverage of 500,000 500,000 rupees to every Indian family.
So with this uh opportunities we as I mentioned there are certain challenges which technology can address.
What are the challenges I mentioned? We do have shortage of specialists. We do have shortage of
radiologists across the country. We do have shortage of health specialists. We have lot of delays that
happens and this translates into heavy incidence of disease. For example, 26% of tuberculosis diagnosis uh
incidents happens in India. How can we ensure that we can eradicate such diseases and this would require

massive application of of uh technology and AI. Then non-communicable diseases has also gone up. The
burden of non-communicable diseases. We see the annual incidence of stroke being very high. How can we
use technology? How can we analyze data? How can we monitor healthare? How can we support uh
primary and secondary healthcare uh service providers to be able to address these challenges. So we are
seeing a huge growth in number of startups who are trying to solve these problems. Like for example we
have a startup called cure cure.ai which is offering its services to several countries. They have served more
than 20 million they have done 20 million TB screenings. We have Intelly Health which is like developing an
AI based solution for for detecting disease like epilepsy. They are trying to develop an AI application to
analyze EEGs so that they can detect early onset of Alzheimer's and other diseases. We have forus which is
trying to solve the eye care challenges that we have early diagnosis of of cataract. How it can be done even
diagnosis of cataract can be done by analyzing the images that are taken by a lowcost mobile phone in a
rural area by a by a last mile health worker asha worker as we call it. Ekaare as I mentioned Vikalp is here
like how they have digitized and created Aishwan bharat health accounts and how the EMR solution that
they have developed can transform the way hospitals analyze their data how they can analyze what kind of
patients are there what kind of cases they are they are getting and how the electronic health records can
benefit not only the patients who are whose records are there but even the overall public health
management system has been there for a long time with their solution for diagnosing breast cancer is well
known. So what we need to do in order to like move up the chain in order to move up and in order to
ensure that these solutions don't remain isolated proof of concept pilots how do we scale up these
solutions? How do we make these solutions ubiquitous? How do we deploy these solutions at scale? How
do we ensure that every block, every primary health center, every community health center, every health
service provider, health service provider gets access to s services. To do this there are certain challenges
that we have which we need to address. What are these challenges? For example, what we are seeing is
that the demand for compute is growing very rapidly. And when we mean compute when we AI compute
especially the the high powered chips which uh only one company Nvidia has a monopoly the cost of
accessing these chips to analyzing the data that you give get even to do inferencing or model training is very
very high. How do you ensure that we are able to make compute available, accessible at a low cost to all
those who need it? Data in fact becomes very important. How do you make data available? We do are we
are a large country. We generate a lot of data but making this data available to those who read it. For
example, uh hospitals like as generate a lot of X-ray images, MRI images. But can we make this anonymized
data sets available to those who are wanting to train models or those who are wanting to build
applications? That remains a challenge. It requires digitization. It requires annotation. It requires having
policies for enabling access to data sets. We saw how Mayo Clinic has billions of data sets. If such data sets,
imaging data sets are made available to our startups and entrepreneurs and researchers, maybe they can
create solutions which will be best better than any anywhere else. Similarly, disease incidence data, we
have blood reports. There are so many labs across the country which do blood blood tests for various
parameters whether it's a lal path labs or other network of path labs that we have. Can we kind of build up
a system in which anonymized data sets with regard to such testing is made available on a platform which is
accessible for those who are building healthcare solutions. So again that data becomes a key element for
being able to solve these challenges. Then of course models we are seeing huge growth in in building
models. Models is something AI models is something that have become in many ways commoditized. There
are models, there are large language models and there are sectoral models in healthcare like the one I
mentioned about the EEG analysis for detecting Alzheimer's or epilepsy. So there are sectoral models also
that are coming in which which can transform development of such applications. Talent is again something
which we are proud of. We are uh India's in fact biggest strength in the whole AI story is our our human
capital, our talent that we have. In fact, the Stanford AI index 2025 report rates India as the number one
country when it comes to AI skill penetration. If you look at the GitHub repository, the number of AI
projects which are contributed to there, Indians have a share of 20% of AI AI projects on the GitHub
repository. So we do have talent. Our our engineers are part of almost every global company which is which
is there and they are building applications models for the whole big big tech companies. How can we ensure
that this talent is given an opportunity given funding given uh compute data set so that they can incubate
these companies within India and build solutions that can solve problems not only of India but for the
world. So this is the problem statement that we have and to do this we are implementing the AI mission in
India which is trying to address some of these challenges. So the AI mission was approved by the

government with a with a outlay of around $1.2 2 billion last year and March and what is trying to do is to
trying to address all the bottlenecks all the challenge that we have. What all we have been able to do so far
was one of the biggest challenge as was mentioned was uh was the availability of AI compute which is
accessible at a low cost to all those who need it. So when the when the program was approved last year the
goal was to make 10,000 GPUs available a minimum of 10,000 GPUs. Today we have been able to make
38,000 GPUs which are available to the Indian AI ecosystem researchers, startups, industry uh students at a
low cost of rupees 65 which is less than a dollar per GPU per hour against the global norm of around $2.5 to
$3 per GPU. And this will help us in some way to address the gap that we have. Of course, if you look at the
US and the other companies, they are building hundreds of thousands of GPU farms, AI factories as they call
it. So, we are trying to go ahead and we hope that by the end of the year, we will have at least 50,000. As
we go forward, the demand goes up, the number of investors who are investing in this, especially with
companies like Jio announcing 1 gawatt data center, other companies to invest in India, we will be able to
address this gap of compute capacity that exists in India. The other big challenge was data sets. For this we
have built a platform called AI kosh. What AI kosh does is that it makes data sets across domains both from
public sector and private sector available on a common platform wherein researchers and end users can go
and access these data sets and use it for training models or building applications. Doing that AI kosh also
provides access to models. Today we have almost 2,000 data sets there which is growing every day. It was
set up only in March this year. We have almost 240 models and we are also putting tools therein for data
anonymization, bias detection, algorithmic out auditing, ethical framework, stress testing. So all these tools
are also being built up being put up on the AI coach platform along with providing a sandbox environment
wherein those who want to try out these applications or train models can access compute data sets at one
point and uh and uh build their solutions. Then we have a scheme under program called building Indian
foundation model. Today all the foundation models that are there are uh we don't have an Indian LLM to to
to use for our requirements. We are dependent on the LLMs that have been trained in the western
countries and very often these models have inherent biases because Indian data sets are not there. They're
not trained in Indian context. So government of India is supporting startups like Serwam, socket, GNI, GAN
and day before yesterday we announced eight more such initiatives. One from IID Bombay was and the
team from IIT Bombay is trying to build up a sovereign AI model which will be a 1 trillion parameter model
and we are giving them funding support of almost in excess of $100 billion almost 988 crores. So there are
12 initiatives that are currently ongoing and we hope that by the time when we host the summit we will
have an Indian LLM. Indian LLM will be a voice-based LLM which will ride on projects like Bhashini. I see
Amitab my colleague here. So Bhashini has enabled seamless textto text speechto text translations and
ultimately the way India's AI will be built will be a voice powered tool wherein people will be able to access
services by voice command and they will be able to ask queries with regard to whatever they are seeking.
For example, if there is a woman in a tribal area in Bihar and if she is wanting to know what she should do
for a child which is suffering with diarrhea, she could be able to put her query in her mother tongue
whichever it is and get a solution back on voice. And this solution will work not only on smartphones and
devices with connectivity. They will should should work even on low cost 2,000 rupees feature phones
which can be used to call up toll-free numbers and give a query. A farmer should be able to put a query with
regard to agricultural practices, with regard to how much fertilizer he should use, how much pesticide he
should use, how much water he should use for irrigation, where he should sell his crop and ensure that gets
the right information. A small businessman, MSME or a small retailer should also be able to get information
with regard to what should be his product risk, where should he buy his raw materials, where should he sell,
how he should optimize his operations and get this information in real time. So Indian LLM will ensure all of
it and when that happens the efficiency and productivity of every sector of the economy will grow just like
uh LLM tools that we use chat GPT or anything that we use help us being able to do more with less. I'm able
to save time. I'm able to read documents faster. I'm able to create documents faster faster. I'm able to
analyze papers. I'm able to read balance sheet faster. And if that makes me efficient, if similar efficiency is
transferred to the other sectors especially agriculture, industry, manufacturing and those sectors become
efficient and productive, we will be able to add to the national economy. We will be able to add to per
capita income and that will be our road to vixid bharat that would help us move up from a $4 trillion
economy to a $30 trillion economy. So that's what is the potential of building this foundation models which
you are doing. Then the other key pillar under the India AI mission is of course whenever we build AI
solutions the whole requirement remains of how do we build a safe and trustworthy AI applications and

models. How do we ensure that the solutions which are being built conform to the responsible AI principles.
India's AI policy prioritizes innovation over restraint. It kind of provides for innovation but at the same time
it also provides for guardrails to to limit user harm. While we are all for building applications using AI which
can result in benefits but the at the same time challenges like deep fakes, challenges like misinformation,
challenges like use for AI for causing any harm has to be limited. So we are coming up with our AI
governance guidelines and we are building several tools which will ensure that we will be able to test these
applications for confirmance to safe and trustworthy principles. We have tools for machine learning. We
have tools for privacy preservation. We have tools for deep fake detection. We have tools for watermarking
AI generated content which will ensure that all that AI is being developed conforms to the broader
principles of responsible AI development. We are also building based on these common elements that we
are providing compute, data sets, models and tools. On the top of it, we are providing support to develop
applications like how do we how do we build applications across sectors which can transform healthcare. So
we we did an innovation challenge and we have identified 30 applications across the five domains of
healthcare, agriculture, climate change, governance and learning disabilities. And these solutions are now
being scaled up and deployed across the country. And in order to support the entire ecosystem, we are also
focusing on building talent, investing in talent. Even though we have a talent advantage, but in order to
survive, in order to retain that advantage, we do need to invest in skilling. For that we are providing
fellowships across say across undergrad level, post-graduate, PhD level for developing AI based applications
in our universities and colleges. And these fellowships are not limited to only engineering students. These
fellowships are available to students doing medicine, law, liberal arts, commerce. So because AI is
horizontal cuts across domains and there will be applications of AI in almost all sectors. Then we also
building data labs acural areas tier two tier cities because there will lot of jobs which will come up in the
data economy and training people to become data annotators, data trackers, data analysts and data
scientists is a is an important focus area. Then we also have a provision for providing funding support to
startups. How do we provide funding support to startups when they need the most like at the seed stage at
the preed stage? So we are setting up a fund of funds and working with accelerators incubators to provide
funding support. So this is the whole ecosystem for ensuring that we are able to catch up if not leaprog to
be able to to match what the countries like US and China are doing. AI coach I've already mentioned we
have data sets and we have lots of we have specialized data sets for healthare in this whole domain so

The AI impact summit as I mentioned will be on 19th and 20th February 2026. In fact, Mr. Gupta told me
that this event is also kind of a pre-event for the impact summit because what we are also doing is that as
we build up the momentum towards the impact summit, we are hosting several events across India and

showcasing solutions, attending panels, curating panels at the impact summit and the impact summit as you
know is the followup of the summit that happened in Bletchley Park in UK in November 2023 wherein safety
was the main focus safety and security were the main focus from there the last year in fact soul Korea
hosted the innovation and security summit and this year we had the AI action summit in Paris which was cohosted by India and France and therein the whole charter was widened to ex to expand the discussions
about AI from just safety and security to also action which included building sustainable AI, building public
interest AI, focusing on governance, focusing on future of work, focusing on building safe and trustworthy
AI. So this is the AI impact summit that we'll be hosting. We'll see will will be at a very big scale. We are

course is to is to presenting India's AI proess to the world bringing the narrative of global south into the
whole AI discourse. Presently the entire AI story is controlled by a few countries and a few companies. How
do you make AI more inclusive? How do you democratize access to AI resources especially compute data
sets models? How do we ensure that a researcher sitting in Kenya or or Morocco also has the access to
similar level of GPUs as anybody sitting in the valley? So that's the key focus area. We have enabled that
democratization of data sets and compute in India. Can a similar model be built for the world? Can we build
a CERN for AI? Can we ensure that globally we collaborate, we fund, we build up an institutional framework
for enabling access to AI resources for everyone? How do we develop indigenous AI solutions? How do we

the domain of education, agriculture or healthcare, can we build a repository of AI solutions, open-source AI
solutions that can be available for any country to use, adapt, customize and deploy? That's the challenge
that we have. Can we work together towards laying down a global governance framework of AI? AI as a
technology is inherently a global technology. models are developed in one geography, applications in some
other geography, use cases are in some other geography. How do we ensure that we set up benchmarks,
we set up standards for governance or ethical AI which is common across. So anyone developing an AI
solution should be able to confirm to that. But this would require a lot of collaboration. There are several

are several forums on this we are being debated we are part of those discussions but can we bring that to a

can India lead the voice of the global south in ensuring an equitable and inclusive AI ecosystem so that's the
key objectives that we have the themes that we have focused on like when you're talking of impact impact

&gt;&gt; Thank you sir. I now invite Dr. Richa Bagla, Principal Security Finance, Accounts and Treasuries
Government of Maharashtra to address the gathering. A very good morning honorable chief secretary sir
government of Maharashtra. Honorable Professor Karandikar, Secretary DST Government of India.
Professor Shirish Kedar, Director IIIT Bombay. Shri Amed Shukla, JSMEA, Director IT, Government of India,
distinguished dignitaries on the DAS, distinguished faculty members, domain experts, researchers, industry
leaders, esteemed delegates, ladies and gentlemen. It is a pleasure to be a part of AI for science conclave a
significant prevent to the India AI impact summit 2026. I compliment IT Bombay and the organizing team for
creating this important platform where science technology governance and policy converge. I would like to
now share my views on role of AI in governance and public finance. In government, especially in the domain
of public finance, we are custodians of public money, public trust, and public outcomes. Digital platforms
today are no longer just systems of record. They are systems of intelligence. Our integrated financial
management system or IFMS as we call it in short is one such mission critical platform. It processes salaries,
pensions, receipts, payments, budget execution and accounting for the state. IFMs handles largecale
realtime sensitive financial data making it an ideal candidate for responsible and targeted use of AI. uh
there are various opportunities which I would like to uh throw in front of the participants which can be used
in the IFMS ecosystem. For instance, uh each government needs to ensure timely cash flows for its various
functions for its various schemes. So one area where AI can definitely help is predictive analytics for cash
flow, revenue trends and expenditure patterns. It can help with early warning systems for abnormal
transactions, fraud detection and risk profiling. A AI can greatly improve and expedite automated
compliance and audit readiness, thus reducing manual intervention. [snorts] And not just this decision
support systems for budget formulation and outcome based monitoring but also intelligent grievous and
exception handling for faster service delivery are some of the key areas in which AI can be a strong
backbone for IFMS and that is being explored by government of Maharashtra at this very moment. These
are not futuristic concepts. With the right data governance, security frameworks and institutional capacity,
AI can meaningfully engage with augmented decision making in IFMS without compromising the
accountability. I would now like to share my thoughts on AI for science and evidence-based policy. AI for
science is equally relevant to government. Scientific methods powered by AI help us move towards
evidence-based policym. Whether it is forecasting fiscal stress, evaluating scheme outcomes, or assessing
long-term sustainability, AI enables us to shift from reactive governance to anticipatory governance, which
is very important in today's time and age. However, I would like to emphasize that in government systems,
AI must remain assistive, transparent, and auditable. Human oversight, explanability, and ethical use are
non-negotiable, especially in financial systems that impact millions of citizens. Collaboration with
institutions from point of view of government is very very important and critical like IIT, Cedak, NIC, Matei
and so on. We can create policy relevant research with real world impact which is especially important for a

dynamic state like Maharashtra. We look forward to stronger collaboration between government and
technology institutions to co-create solutions that are scalable, secure and citizen centric. I'm confident that
deliberations in this conclave contribute significantly to shaping India's AI journey not only in science and
research but also in governance, public finance and service delivery. I once again thank I Bombay for the
invitation and for hosting this timely and very impactful conclave. I wish the conference great subject
success and look forward to meaningful deliberations that translate into action. I wish you all the success.

so let's do the second part first which is uh AI for engineering. What can AI do for engineering? Just like we
can think of AI for physics, AI for biology, AI for chemistry, AI for astronomy. What's AI for engineering? So
first point I want to make is and this is an important point and it came up several times this morning in
various speakers. They said oh science is physics, chemistry, math. Uh you know science is hypothesis
experiment validation and disprove the hypothesis and make a new engineering has its own science and it's
very different from this kind of hypothesis-based science. So engineering science is specialized foundational
disciplinary knowledge tools and techniques and frameworks for various engineering fields thinks very
simply it's our sort of third year fourth year undergraduate curriculum in any department of engineering or
first or second year graduate courses in engineering they don't look like I mean they take from science they

pointing to so AI techniques have enormous potential to transform these engineering science fields but it
will happen in its own way. It won't happen the way it will happen for physics or astronomy. It will happen it
the way it will happen for our fields likely to ex far exceed far exceed the impact of high performance
computing on engineering science. So the last such big revolution in engineering science was CF you know
sort of high performance computing. So we went I mean I grew up in a time when the answer was a closed
form formula because that's basically what you could do. We had slide rules. So you know you really didn't
want to do much more than close formula. Well we changed that dramatically in the 80s and 90s with HPC.
This thing that's coming with AI is probably going to be 10 10 times bigger change in engineering science.

interaction. The HCI is is an insufficient model for what needs to happen. It's H human machine
collaboration. So I'm going to talk about that. um be uh a couple more ideas before I sort of talk about this
collaboration. There are two different axes here. One is automation and second is autonomy. Now we use
these two words most often in a confused manner. We sort of you know interchangeably say automation
we say autonomy but they are actually very different. So I'm laying out here. So automation is really who
does what. So machine level one is machine offers decision choices to the human. Machine says okay here
are the things. Human makes a choice. I want to go left. I want to go to right. I want to drive at 55 miles an
hour. So automation is giving choices to the humans and human is making the the decision. Level two is
machine gets a little bit more uh automated. It executes with human approvals. It will do the thing and all it
is asking for is do you approve me to do this? Third is machine executes if no human vetos. Okay. So here
it's only a veto power. So I have to proactively say machine please don't do this. Otherwise the machine is
going to go and go do this. Level four is machine executes and then says you know I just did this. I did this
for you. Okay. And then finally machine executes fully automated without informing us. So that's the
highest level of automation. Now what is autonomy? There are two different um ideas on autonomy. There
is the human autonomy like me as an autonomous entity. Right? I mean I'm not talking about that. I'm
talking about technical autonomy of sort of you know engineered systems. So technical autonomy is level
one lowest level is deterministic transparent and closed system. So your theat is kind of that right. So it
maintains you know your your temperature in your in your home. It is it is deterministic. It's transparent
and you know exactly what it does and that's the level of autonomy it has. U second is deterministic nontransparent closed system. So it's still determinist does exactly it's the same thing every time but it's not
transparent. You don't know how it does it because it's too complicated but it's closed. So it doesn't you
know it does what it does and it's in a closed box and that's that's about it. Level three is non-deterministic
non-transparent and closed. AI systems are getting there. So they are non-deterministic. They are nontransparent and at this time they're kind of closed. Okay, at this time they're not seeking out new resources
and new information. Although that's we are on the verge of changing breaking the level three. Level four is
adaptable but closed. So it's so in this case now you are saying that okay this system the the engineered
system is adaptable. It's adapting to the environment to the changes to what's going on but it's closed. It's
not seeking new resources. And finally adaptable and open. So when you think about the combination of
adaptable and open with ASI that's where the you know if you believe in all of that that's when you need to
really really worry because if it can gather resources the paperclip example if it can gather resources then
we are in trouble. But if we can sort of stay within up to closed systems where there are limits on what
resources the AI system can command deeer then I think maybe we are going to be okay. So the big issue
for uh human machine collaboration is on the x-axis you have the technical autonomy and on the y-axis you
have the automation. Where are we going to place this future AI engineered systems in this diagram? We
surely will worry a lot if it is in the top right corner. Okay, we are very happy and we are very used to being

other. So this is the classic you know Alice has a belief model of Bob and Bob has a model of Alice and they
both have belief about the world and without these models we really couldn't collaborate. Now many times
these beliefs actually are inconsistent right so I believe in something and you know Romesh believes in
something else and the two beliefs are completely uh inconsistent in which case our collaboration become
very difficult but if our beliefs are closer and closer and that happens through collaboration maybe we work
together to achieve something great that's how humans do it for machines and humans to do this we are
going to have to have something similar where the human will have a model of the machine and I think
that's almost like you know almost surely going to happen because we humans are built to make these
models. Our brains are designed to make models of things that we interact with. So we will have like an
internal representation of these machines. The question is will the machine have an internal representation
of us in a way that we want? And so I think that's the piece we're going to have to design into these systems
as a as a thought partner. So Alice and machine beliefs about the world and beliefs about each other. uh so
human should have a mental model of the machine, machine should have a model of the human and the
model should help predict actions of the partner and in in fact bring the beliefs closer to each other. So that
is going to be a very key piece of this human machine collaboration or these these models and you know
there's a lot more detail than I'm showing you. So you can read the paper but there is collaborative planning

design for this future. Second piece that I want to sort of highlight and this is now coming to an end of my
my talk is uh what is called inferential social learning framework. One of the things that humans really and I
kind of hinted excel at is thinking about other people either implicitly or explicitly. And uh a great example is
that and I I'll say this to you and you have to think about it and see if it agrees with your intuition. Anytime
two people interact anytime we very quickly switch mode between teacher and learner. So right now I'm
standing in front of you and I'm your I'm kind of in the teacher mode and you're in the student mode. As
soon as it becomes Q&amp;A which we won't do because we don't have time, you will ask me a question
and then you become my teacher and I become then the student in a in a interpersonal interaction. This
switch between learner and and a teacher is many many times in 10 15 minutes. Okay, we go from one to
the other very quickly. But there's a whole theory that's built on top of this observation called inferential
social learning. So characterize the interactions between two agents one who wants to learn learner and
the one who wants to teach as the result of mutual mental state reasoning and planning. Now you can use
that between the machine and the human as this inferential social learning. And you know the picture on
the right is basically to say that as the two interact their overlap about what they know about each other
and what they know about the world increases allowing for better and better collaboration. So there is like
a whole bunch of cognitive science that goes underneath the picture I'm showing you. But these are the
kinds of things that we are going to need to have humans interact in a collaborative manner with these
machines that we are we are unleashing on the world. So these are ex these are sort of specific techniques
like probabistic models of cognition, theory of mind, resource rationality and probabilistic programming. So

SPEAKER / SESSION: AI for Social Impact： MeitY Leads the Charge at ESTIC 2025 [2pAzbEJsUhQ].en
====================================
Artificial intelligence is reshaping economies and societies across the globe. By 2030, AI is expected to
contribute nearly $19.5 trillion to the world economy. For India, the opportunity is transformative with
close to $1 trillion that could be added to our GDP by 2035. Beyond economics, AI promises empowerment,
efficiency, and inclusion, redefining how we deliver healthcare, education, agriculture, governance, and
industry. It is this transformative potential that positions AI as a key driver of India's march towards Vixith
Bharat 2047. India recognized early the need to create a strong AI foundation. The national strategy for
artificial intelligence in 2018 set the road map followed by centers of excellence and the national program
on AI. In 2024, the India AI mission was launched with an allocation of over 10,000 cr rupees. Built on seven
pillars, compute capacity, foundation models, data sets, application development, startups, skills, and safe
and trusted AI. The mission has already delivered results. National compute power has crossed 38,000
GPUs. 31 AI labs have been established in tier 2 and tier 3 cities. 12 foundation models being developed and
more than 3,000 data sets are available on AI Kosh, India's unified AI data set platform. Under the visionary
leadership of honorable Prime Minister Shri Narendra Modi, India has placed artificial intelligence at the
heart of its development journey. Guided by the principles of inclusion and trust, AI in India is not just about
technology but about people. Initiatives like digital India Bhashani are breaking language barriers across 22
Indian languages. Bhat gen India's sovereign multilingual foundation model ecosystem is creating
Indiacentric models and solutions that reduce dependence on foreign systems from precision agriculture to
digital classrooms and AIdriven healthcare the technology is already transforming lives ensuring no citizen is

left behind a revolution of this scale needs skilled minds and bold innovators initiatives like India AI future
skills Future Skills Prime, Visvehia PhD Scheme, and AI for all are preparing millions for the jobs of
tomorrow. Over 5 million citizens have already completed the AI for all course in 11 languages. Startups are
being empowered with computer access, mentorship, and funding to innovate across sectors. On the global
stage, India is shaping the future of AI governance as founding member and chair of the global partnership
on AI, leading efforts for safe, human- ccentric and trusted AI worldwide. As India moves towards 2047,
artificial intelligence will remain a cornerstone of national transformation. With sustained investment,
innovation and global leadership, AI will empower citizens, strengthen communities and drive growth
across every sector. Together, we are building a future where India not only adapts to change but leads it
with AI for all, AI for India and AI for the world. The power of artificial intelligence. The promise of
innovation through every algorithm. Igniting the spirit of innovation. The inaugural ESTIC 2025. The
emerging science technology and innovation conclave. Imagine, innovate and inspire for Vix Bharat 2047.

From grassroots administration to the helm of India's digital governance, Abishek Singh has charted a

Administrative Service, he has shaped [music] policy and delivered impact across sectors from rural
infrastructure and [music] disaster response to education, health care and urban reform. In 2025, [music]
he was entrusted with one of the nation's most pivotal digital roles, director general of the National [music]

[music] Technology. In his current roles, he spearheads efforts to build India's AI ecosystem, including
development of compute infrastructure, data sets, homegrown models, and AI [music] governance
frameworks. His work ensures that innovation isn't just cutting edge, but inclusive, [music] secure, and
accessible to all. At the heart of his vision lies a uniquely Indian digital future. One that is built not only on
adoption of global AI models but development of indigenous AI [music] solutions that reflect India's

very very fast forward and can result in lot of efficiency and productivity gains. [snorts] So with this in
objective what we did like the India AI mission which was approved by the government last year which we
in March of 24 it kind of focused on that how how what can the government do to ensure that we are able
to realize our AI potential because it was felt that while we do have the talent and we do have the expertise

and we do have people who have the skills to build AI products and services but there's something that is
ensuring that we are lagging much much behind China and the US and the delta is too huge to cover. So for
that we tried to identify we did consultations with industry with academia with researchers with AI
stakeholders and we realized that there are several bottlenecks like the amount of money that we spend for
R&amp;D was much much lesser compared to a China and the US do the the availability of compute was a
big bottleneck because till last year we had only about about 600 odd GPUs that was available for our entire
ecosystem. Then the amount of funding that was required for startups and all that was like a big big limiting
factor. So to address these gaps the AI mission was conceptualized and the government has showed funding
from all the verticals as seven pillar strategy that we have. So briefly I will touch upon that. So idea was to
make compute available and accessible to those who need it. So we did a very in interesting private
sectorled incentive mechanism for setting up compute infrastructure and today we do have almost 40,000
GPUs much lesser compared to the AI factories that are coming up world over but at least 40,000 GPUs are
available at a much much lower cost government underwrites almost 40% of the jump cost so those who
need it they are able to get it at around 65 rupees which is less than a dollar per GPU per hour compared to
the global prices of $2.5. And then we also realized that while there are a lot of foundation models, LLMs
which have been built in the US and now of course open source solutions in China but India did not have an
LLM of its own. So we do aspire and we do believe that we have the capability of doing so. So we are
funding almost 12 such initiatives for building Indian LLMs and SLMs in various sectors like healthcare and
material science and the rest. So we do believe that two pro two initiatives especially one led by Saram and
one led by Bharat Jin in IT Bombay they are at very almost near and before the summit that we're hosting in
February we should be able to announce an Indian LLM which will be primarily a voice-based LLM which will
be able to provide services which I was referring to to farmers and to retailers and to students and to
people needing healthare then we have also like one another link that was observed was that even though
we a large country and we've done a lot of digitization over the years but one key problem that all AI
stakeholders felt that access to data so we are setting up a data sets platform AI coach because data is like
inherent other than compute and talent without data you can't build AI applications and models so we have
set up India data sets platform called AI kosh which has got like today 3500 data sets from both public
sector and private sector and kind of being ramped up as we speak and uh and we do believe that this will
become like one place which will help uh entrepreneurs and researchers to train models and to build AI
applications. Then we also launched an application development initiative so that we can identify certain
applications which can be scaled up across country especially in education in climate change in agriculture
in healthcare in governance. So we do have around 30 applications that we are currently working with state
governments and government departments to scale them up and to deploy that for example an AI assistant
for farmers an AI tool for diagnosing tuberculosis scatteract diabetic retinopathy because very often we do
have machines X-ray machines and radiology machines in the rural areas but sometimes we don't have
qualified radiologists and doctors so that problem can easily be solved with significant level of accuracy by
the AI machines and AI bots that's currently being done. Then we have a future skills program because what
we do believe is that even though we do have the talent advantage but if we don't invest in talent enough
we will be losing that advantage. So we are providing funding support for research for AI projects at
undergrad postgrad and research level. So that's currently happening. We also are setting up data labs
across the country because we do need to train people to become data scientists to data annotators data
analysts because those jobs are also being created. So especially the ITIs and the poly techchnics therein we
are trying to produce data scientists and data annotators so that they can take up those jobs. We do have a
safe and trusted pillar because it's all whenever we talk of AI it's always also accompanied by that how do
we ensure that we do have the capacity to ensure that all AI applications are safe are trustworthy are
responsible conformed to the ethical principles. So under the safe and trustworthy pillar, we have set up AI
safety institute and we are part of the global network of safety institutes wherein we are developing tools
for bias mitigation, for ethical AI certification, for privacy preservation, for machine learning, for detecting
deep fakes, for labeling AI generated content. So that we are working with research institutions to develop
such tools which will be available as a global as a AI commons for the entire ecosystem. Then we have a
startup financing pillar in which we are working with VCs and funds to come out with a fund of funds model
wherein we will be able to co-invest in the startups. So the idea is that identified funds will be make
identifying the AI startups in which they will be investing and the government will just match the
investment at the valuation levels that has been arrived at. So the actual task of identification and working

out the levels will be done by experts and we will kind of just supporting that effort so that the funding is

doing under the AI mission. So I will now quickly come to the impact summit and uh a lot of it will be

one mantra is the impact and we are looking at impact on three key pillars. The way it has been structured
is that the three key pillars on which we are looking at the impact are people planet progress. The people
pillar of course is the most important for a country like India which has such a strong ITI it sector wherein
we'll focus on how AI impacts jobs, how AI impacts employment, the need for reskilling, upskilling and how
we can ensure that we can leverage it for our benefit and for the world's benefit in the days to come. So
that will be the people part. The issues of inclusion will also be covered there. Then the planet is of course
about uh making AI more sustainable because we all know the amount of AI energy the AI is consuming.
How do we build green data centers? How do we identy for AI compute? So that will be one part for making
AI more sustainable and more efficient. But the other part will be like how we can use AI to address climate
challenges. how AI can be used for predicting floods, for predicting extreme heat events and even
something like how AI can be used to make grids more efficient. How do we reduce power energy
transmission and distribution losses? We are already doing several projects in India in that direction. So the
impact of AI on planet will be a very key focus area. And of course progress progress will have two
dimensions. One is like AI for economic growth in the sense that how it leads to productivity gains,
efficiency gains and the second dimension will be AI for social good. how it can help be used to expand
access of services in healthcare, in agriculture, education. So those two dimensions will be there both
economic growth and social good. So that's the structuring that we have and across these three pillars we
have seven themes that we have taken up. There are seven working groups which are chaired by uh by

India and then we have co two co-chairs from different countries mainly one global north and one global
south. So 14 countries are also part of these working groups. The themes are of course AI for economic
growth and social good, democratizing AI resources, inclusion, then safe and trusted AI, human capital of
course on the jobs at the people part, then resilience, innovation and efficiency which will focus on
sustainability and then science. Science has also been taken up like how AI can be used for advancing drug
discovery for using in cutting edge R&amp;D. So those these are seven working groups and these working
groups have already started uh having their meetings and what we do hope is that these working groups
will come out with the outcome statements and outcome documents action plans and this will ultimately
flow into the declaration that will be adopted at the summit. So so this is like the whole structure like the
working groups as I mentioned so their outcomes will go into the adopting the declaration at the summit.
So these are the working group meetings which have already started. In fact almost 60 countries and 20
international organizations have been part of the working group meetings that have already been held. We
are looking at three virtual meetings and one hybrid meeting of the working groups. Then we have like
several flagship events along with the working groups and the summit outcomes and declarations. We also
launched three innovation challenges. an open innovation ch innovation challenge entries of it closed on

AI principles and this UK energy regulatory of gem has issued ethical AI guidelines. uh while Singapore has a
practical AI governance framework. So global directions are very clear that AI must be transparent,
accountable, safe and humanentric. So this uh handbook covers every of these aspects in detail. You can

the DPI. Open, trusted, interoperable, ecosystemled, right? Not closed, not proprietary. And I think that is
the secret behind building trusted technologies or trusted solutions because if you don't have trust, you're
never going to achieve scale. And for me the the DPI story is a success primarily because of the core design
principles around which it was built. And if you look at it abishek that those are also the core principles
around which you and the government is building India's tech stack and overall tech solution right even
India AI mission it's about democratizing access to technology it's democratizing access to the critical

resources compute no other country is doing that it's democratizing access to compute it's democratizing
access to data is democratizing access to skills Right? If you look at of the seven pillars of India AI, that's
what it really is doing for India and therefore enabling India leaprog. So if we can apply those principles of
trusted technology that builds trusted technology to artificial intelligence, I think that's what's going to be
the biggest unlock for scale. How do you create ecosystemled solutions where it's not about one country or
two countries but where has a voice and that's been one of the biggest objectives of this working group
ensuring that everyone's voice is heard right everyone's ideas are heard and the surprising thing was when
we opened it up and when we heard everyone out actually everyone was saying the same thing right there
weren't any big differences. Everyone was talking about literally maybe the language is different but the

specifically for the advancement of women. Uh can you tell us that what are the impact use cases which can

people are left out like women how how does it how do you help them? So you know we talking about AI

participating in the formal economy. Uh the challenge has been there have been three big structural
barriers. One is a barrier on the infrastructure side. Data is one of them. Uh housing mobility safe and
affordable mobility safety. The second big uh uh pillar is around um policy policy some of the policies that
hold women back and the third is supply and demand side barriers. So demand side barriers in companies
around hiring, retention, advancement of women and in each of these pillar there are multiple use cases
but I'll talk about one specifically which we've been thanks to uh the focus by the uh UP government and
the planning department uh we've been able to uh work a lot on the data layer of it. So of the three layer,
the data, compute and the model. On the data side, one of the things we did was we looked at about 100
plus schemes that impact women and collected data from 21 line departments. And the big problem is that
gender disagregated data itself is not available for decision making. When you are making decisions, the
people who are making decisions, they do not have the data at their fingertips. Um so what we did was as
we looked at these 21 line departments collected this information based on that there are now we've been
able to rank 75 districts and created something called women's economic empowerment index and every
district now gets a score and you can see which schemes are being used in which district. So now there is a
uniform data layer available across districts and across departments to be able to say okay here are the
three things that we need to do in order to improve participation of women and there are all of those 100
plus schemes that that are enabling that. Um so that that is something that we have now rolled out and
now at a district level and at a department level we are beginning to act on some of uh some of those um
issues. One of the things I want to talk about is uh for example uh bus conductors, women bus conductors.
There were very few women bus conductors and when some of this data was being presented in some of
the meetings that was highlighted as a point in one year a target and this time um the number of bus
conductors that have been hired is 10 times women bus conductors is 10 times that of last year. They had
to change a lot of processes to enable that. But that is the power of a common data layer that is available
across districts and departments to make uh &gt;&gt; Sure. Thank you Puja. It's really a great example and

departments uh of the central government and potentially state government and ulbs can also use it. These

urban local bodies and uh so and so there are many such examples. Now I'll just tell you one common thing
uh common feature amongst these. it is that uh uh there is most impact using an AI solution when you are
solving a problem which will be very laborious even for the experts to solve. So if experts can solve it easily
then you should not use AI but if there's laborious even for 10 experts then using AI you can have an
impact. Um so for example there are hundreds of thousands of grievances which are being submitted by the
citizens. They may be written by hand. They may be typed. They may be in English or Hindi or any other
language and it has to be uh officer has to read it and classify it according to the content. Now this even
with hundreds of officers is very hard to do because one has to go through the complaint when has to
understand what is being said. Many times the complainant is only giving a rant. It's only a rant against the
government or against a department. So what exactly is the problem? One has to actually read and
understand and then you have to do it in 10 languages and in many kinds of modes. So these kind of things
AI is very good at. So our system within seconds can classify the views and then it is uh according to the
classification it is pushed to the departments. So instead of departments pulling out the complaints out of
50,000 or 100,000 now the complaints is being pushed onto the department. So this is something which
even experts cannot solve human experts cannot solve. So AI actually helps and then it is impactful. Uh the
other example is we did it for one state uh which has around 40 million citizens. So they gave us the data of
the citizens and we had to estimate the income. Now this again if you if you hire experts human experts
looking through the data of 40 million citizens and giving estimates for income it's very hard uh it's very
laborious at least. uh so this is where again AI methods do this uh in various rounds we get feedback we can
do a survey we can improve the model and uh u we can be confident that the income estimation range is
good uh so so I would not suggest AI use of AI for critical and legal problems but for problems which are
laborious to do and there is no criticality there is no legality I think where uh it's best to use AI and scale of
problems in India is huge. So in India we actually keep getting many problems from various government

beside themselves. They are uh largely self-employed. So what there is no aspiration gap. They they they
want to build it. The there are resources. There are so many schemes. Every state has a scheme for a

fundamental principle? It is self-efficacy. If you the the self-efficacy and it can be driven by three parts,
right? One is um uh vicarious through role models. if she can do it in my cluster, in my um means and

these u patients are either asymptomatic or partially symptomatic which means the current rule-based
algorithms that are currently being used might miss these uh patients. So we were able to build a noninvasive lowcost solution that in addition to symptomatic data is also able to capture cough sounds solicited
cough sounds and is able to screen for TB patients which essentially means that now the health workers
that are already going doortodoor need to just collect one additional signal apart from what they were
already collecting. The solution already deployed across 10 states. We have done 200,000 odd inferences
and what the result that we were able to find was there was a 13% additional addition to the already
existing cases because those patients that were flagged by the cough application who are asymptomatic or
partially right so these are some of the real world impact that we've been seeing whether it's increasing the

India. Mr. Ahmed Hnavi AI governance expert from Egypt and Shrimmanas Ranjan Pandas special secretary
electronics and IT government of Odisa and MDAC. We extend a very very warm welcome to all our
esteemed dignitaries. Ladies and gentlemen, as you know, India today stands at a defining moment in its
artificial intelligence journey. Under the vision of the honorable Prime Minister Shinarendra Modi GI, AI has
emerged as a strategic pillar of national development driving better governance, inclusive growth and
global leadership. At the state level, Orisa has moved decisively to integrate AI into governance, service
delivery, and decision- making through the roll out of the Odisa AI policy 2025. The regional impact AI
conference therefore marks a timely and purposeful step as Odisa positions itself as a proactive contributor
to India's growing national AI momentum. On that note, I would now request Shivashal Kumarade Dave,
principal secretary electronics and IT department who has been at the forefront of the state's technology
and innovation agenda and has played a pivotal role in shaping the Odisai policy 2025 to deliver the
welcome address. A very good afternoon to everyone here. Honorable Minister for Electronics and IT, Dr.
MKkesh Mahaling GI Respected Chief Secretary Shri Manojahja Respected Secretary Ministry of Statistics
and Program Implementation Dr. Sarupg Joint Secretary Ministry of Electronics and IT Government of India
Shri Sudich Shvastav Professor Sheput Karmal Director IIT Bhneshwar Dr. Ahmed Hefnavi, Ministry of
Information Communications Technology from Egypt, my colleague Manuspa, senior officials of the
government, distinguished delegates, members of academia, industries, leaders, ladies and gentlemen. It is
an honor and a privilege to welcome you all to the India AI hybrid working group event to the state of Odisa
and to the temple city of Bhineseshwar. Odisa is a land of rich cultural heritage and diversity and today
stands among one of the top performing states of the country both economically and as a frontr runner
across sectors such as technology, manufacturing, mining and innovation. We are pleased that Odisa has
been selected as one of the eight states across the country to host the regional India AI impact summits.
The theme for today's working group is democratizing AI resources which is both very timely and critically
important as India scales it ambitions in artificial intelligence. When we speak of democratizing AI
resources, we are essentially speaking about access, affordability and equity. The key challenge before us is
to ensure that core AI capabilities are not concentrated in the hands of a few but are made available across
governments, startups, academia and industry. Most importantly, the benefits of AI must translate into
better services and outcomes for citizens. AI resources broadly include four elements: compute, data sets,
models, and talent. For Odisa, access to these resources is closely linked to our governance priorities. Our
approach to AI adoption is driven by the objective of improving public service delivery, making governance
more efficient, transparent, and citizencentric. In this context, Odisa is an apt location for discussions on
democratizing AI resources. Over the past one year, we've been working to build a holistic AI ecosystem
that addresses all the four dimensions of AI resources in an integrated manner. In June 2025, Orisa became
the first state in the country to release a dedicated artificial intelligence policy. Alongside this, we launched
the Odisa AI mission on similar lines as the India AI mission, which provides a structured framework for
implementation. The mission focuses on AIdriven applications and data sets, capacity building and scaling,
enabling infrastructure with compute as a key component and the development of models with a particular
emphasis on language technologies on applications and data sets. We are engaging with national init
initiatives such as AI kosh to enable responsible access to shared data sets and to support the development
of governance relevant AI use cases. Capacity building is central to democratizing AI. Through our
partnership with Vadwani Foundation, we have conducted structured AI workshops for government
officials. And over the past one year, more than 100 officials across several departments have been trained,

helping build institutional capacity for informed and effective AI adoption. Access to infrastructure and
compute is another priority area. Odisa is partnering with NASCOM to establish a center of excellence in the
state. We're also working towards enabling access to compute at affordable rates for startups, researchers,
and institutions in alignment with the objectives of the India AI mission. On the models front, our focus is on
language technologies, particularly for Odia. As a low resource language, strengthening Odia language
models is critical to inclusive digital governance and to ensuring last mile delivery of services in a language
that citizens are comfortable with. I would like to conclude by congratulating the India team for organizing
these working group events and for ensuring that these dialogues remain structured and inclusive across
the nation. I'm sure today's deliberations will be a success. We look forward to the insights and
recommendations that emerge from this working group. I also look forward to the India AI impact summit
scheduled in February which will serve as a key platform to bring together insights from these working
groups, showcase emerging use cases and shape the next phase of India's AI journey. Thank you. [applause]

the world are actively grappling with how to ensure that benefits of artificial intelligence are broadly shared
responsibly deployed and aligned with public interest outcomes. India's approach to democratizing AI
resources is anchored in India AI mission. So India AI mission has got basically seven pillars. The first pillar is
India AI compute pillar where apart from developing an indigenous compute infrastructure it is also
intended that AI will be provided as a service to the common man. Second pillar is India AI innovate. So in
India AI innovate basically indigenous LLM models are being developed with domain specific requirements
and even the regional aspirations are taken care of. Then we have got an AI course a course that is very
relevant when we have got our own data sets and they are getting stored there. So this is very important
from part point of view of digital sovereignty and also something where uh global south can also learn. Our
fourth pillar is India AI skilling. So of course you need to skill the manpower when you have to take such
things forward. So skilling is one of the pillars. Then we have got the startup financing. So in startup
financing we are financing the startups in this field of AI. The sixth pillar is the India AI application pillar
where the applications pertaining to AI are being basically brought out. The seventh pillar is safe and
reliable AI which will encourage ethical use of AI. Apart from that, India is also hosting India AI uh sorry AI
impact summit 2026th from 15th to 20th February. Now this summit is based on the theme of Servjan Hai
Servan Sukkai which means well-being for all, happiness for all. This is the first such summit in global south
and here more than 100 countries will be participating. CEOs from leading technological companies will be
there. Researchers, innovators and representative all will be present there. Now coming to the theme of
this working group. This lies at the very heart of India AI impact summit and also at the heart of what India
Indian ethos are. India is the mother of democracy. So anything dealing with democratization is at the core
of our thinking. As in AI become critical driver for economic growth and social transformation. Unequal
access to its foundational resources risk deepening global divide. Through this summit, India is seeking to
galvanize collective action among countries particularly in global south to improve access, reduce cost and
promote shared models for compute, data and AI infrastructure that enable meaningful participation in AI
ecosystem. In this context, I would like to mention that this is the fourth meeting of the working group. The
first three meetings were virtual. First was on 5th of November where the scope of the work was worked of
the working group was finalized. The second meeting was on 28th of November where the convergence
across various ideas were brought about. The third virtual meeting was on 12th of December where the

zero draft of the charter was made and today in the fourth meeting efforts will be made to finalize and finetune the charter. So I would once again like to thank Orisa for hosting this vital working group meeting and
we look forward that discussions during this hybrid working group meeting will be fruitful and beneficial to
the entire global community. Thank you very much. [applause] &gt;&gt; Thank you Shri Shivas. Ladies and

discussions on uh or in the working group that we are in, you will hear Egypt's voice speaks often about
sovereignty and contextualization. There is a philosophy behind this, a philosophy that guides our own
national AI strategy and one that I believe should guide this working group. It is simply this is what we do.
We look outward at the international landscape. We study the global trends. We learn from the
breakthroughs happening in Silicon Valley, in Europe and here in Asia. We respect the global state of the art
but we look onward as well. We deeply understand our own issues. We look at our own infrastructure
challenges, our unique languages, our specific economic priorities in agriculture, health and education. And
finally, we come up with our own approach. We don't copy paste solutions. We adapt them. We forgo a
path that is informed by the word but designed for our reality. The charter that the working group is
working on now must be the vehicle that allows every nation to be exactly that. It must give us the global
resources, the compute, the data frameworks, the financing so that we can solve our own problems in our
own way. Let us work together today to ensure this document honors that spirit global cooperation for local
selfdetermination. Thank you very much. [applause] &gt;&gt; Thank you so much uh Mr. Nav'i for your

sure that the error rates are within control. We do three rounds of validation to make sure the data is of the
highest quality possible. We're obviously very worried about model collapse, right? Like that is that is
always something that keeps you up at night, especially when you're working with these big clients. Um and

Uh so let us look at the evolution of products right so the products have evolved from physical products that
came with programmed intelligence then the last 20 years we saw digital products that became better with
feedback right if you click on a search result take a YouTube recommendation that becomes better over
time and now we are heading towards what I call ecosystem products right think of a whole smart city is
one product and that is the level of complexity we are going to be able to achieve in the next 20 years. So
the idea is a smart city is a product. The whole telecom network is a product, not just a SIM card or a phone.
Think of a entire manufacturing unit with the pre and the post is also a product. Think of the entire
agriculture ecosystem of a state as a product. Not just a drone, not just a pesticide, but the entire
ecosystem as a product, right? Uh the entire healthcare ecosystem of a country or the whole e-commerce
platform, right? Like So these are the new ideas of product. Amazon does not sell a product anymore.
Amazon is a product right and that is how we need to think about uh this thing. So what is lacking? Why are
we not able to build this? What kind of a new skill set we need? Right? So my favorite example is electricity.
If you think about how I was talking to Rajes this morning, how do we have electricity? If somebody
invented a bulb, somebody invented a wire, somebody invented a socket or a switch. If we all did bottomup
innovation of individual things, will we have electricity? What is the secret source that we have electricity is
not that we have bulbs and fans and fridges. It is the grid that exists. Anybody can produce energy, put it on
the grid. Anybody can consume energy and uh in a very simple way. Right? So the interface is simple, use
cases are many. And now if you innovate bulbs it'll make sense because you have the grid but without the
grid all those innovations will go to waste. That mindset is very important. Who will create and own the grid
so that everybody can innovate on top. Right? So here's an example and we have seen news of you know

that unless somebody builds a grid we will not get this right so we have the bottom of capabilities the key is

to integrate so integration is the theme for the next 20 years AI is done we know how to do AI integration is
still a missing bit right so let me show some examples in agriculture so this is the kind of call we get every uh
every day Lance Foundation. All right. So he's speaking in Hindi. He's sayingology. So English very specific
question. We know the location. Problem is we can only handle 800 calls per day across India across
languages. We don't have NF experts. This is a very important use case for AI in agriculture which is to give
very precise advisory to every farmer in the country. Right? So we understand the world from a domain
perspective. How do we think of it from a systems perspective? So what I teach now is take a problem like
that. Right? And take the core problem in every domain. Don't take a side problem. Take a core problem in
every domain. So this is the core problem. We talked about the core problem in security. Now we're talking
about the core problem in agriculture. And we start thinking about the workflows and we say first what do
we need? We need our system to understand speech in all languages. Convert it to intent. What crop what
issue you want to handle? We want to create an AI expert. Who will create that? Not a data scientist but all
the AI experts or agriculture experts have to come together. We need to put together a you know
knowledge graph of agriculture. Not text of agriculture. Knowledge graph which crop which stage how
much water how much nitrogen. Then we need each farmer's profile to be built, right? So we need to know
what is the polygon, what is the location, what is the weather, soil type today, what is your moisture
content, right? And then we need all the sensors integrated and then we'll be able to give a very
personalized and contextualized advisory to every part, right? So what is the missing link? Again, we have all
the individual capabilities. We are teaching knowledge graphs, we are teaching databases, we are teaching
IoT, we are teaching you know supply chain but we are not integrating them right and not building the
platforms. So platformization will lead to intact and that is the key message here and this is the
fundamental problem. So this is the new thing we want to learn as students. Don't think of AI sitting
separately somewhere. Think of workflow as a main unit and now AI is part of the workflow. Right?
Operations is part of the workflow. Data is part of the workflow. Sensors is part of the workflow. So how do
we become system thinkers like that? So we can build systems like this. Right? So workflow mindset is very
important. Proactive intelligence. So now imagine a farmer what is the first thing he does in a on a day? He
goes and takes a p you know he surveys the crop right. So now he takes a picture of a crop. One AI says
what is the crop? What is the stage? Another AI model says what is the problem with the crop? If it says
water problem, what should I be able to do? Not tell the farmer that there is a water problem. Control the
tube well and say I need to run the tube well for 30 minutes. Why? Because I know the current water
moisture content. I know the predicted weather. I know how much water this crop needs because of the
knowledge. Right? So all this has to come together to give me the ability to control these systems. And if it's
a nutrition problem, I can send a drone. If it's a pest problem, I can send a drone. I don't even have the
farmer to need to take a picture. Satellites should be able to do it. We are spending so much money sending
satellites. What are they doing? And we can have a drone based farming all over India. This is what I mean
by reimagine. So reimagine agriculture. If you have drones, if you have satellite, if you have sensors, if you
have operations, if you have AI, if you have cloud, can we reimagine agriculture? Right? And that is the new
skill we want to develop. And why is this not happening again? Because we are funding a thousand startups
in agriculture, each one building their own orange box, but there is no grid. You understand? Without the
grid, the bulbs and the switches are useless. And that is the new skill we want to start thinking about. Right?
Integration is the next innovation. Not more AI, better AI, right? We can always do better AI. Uh but uh all
that. So now the operating system mindset says that think about all the events in agriculture. Think about
all the you know AI models in agriculture. Think about all the knowledge of agriculture. Think about all the
content of agriculture. Think about all the digital twin of every farm in agriculture. And now can you in real
time without a human in the loop without Excel and emails uh directly produce advisories, right? We should
ban the export of wheat because something is happening, right? We should put more tomatoes from here
to there because demand is high. All this should be run with AI today. like electricity runs automatically and
we are amazed that it runs. I should be able to do all this right. Education, one of our uh most important uh
social themes. What is missing in our education system today is the the fundamental principle of education
is what we call master learning. I should not teach you what you are not ready to learn. Right? That means
you have finished all the prerequisites and I should not let you move forward if you have not mastered the
current concept. Now this is not possible in the current education system. We have a one-sizefits-all mass
