now I'd like to go to professor Chakravarti for his thoughts &gt;&gt; yeah now see this inclusivity is not a
very simple word it's not just a a word which has a nice clear meaning and we can just use it like you said
like a switch it's not something like that see When we look at especially AI, there are two basic definitions
for AI. One is that AI is the development of algorithms and techniques that mimic human behaviors. The
other is AI is the development of techniques and algorithms that perform a task which was traditionally
performed better by human beings. If you look at these two there are two very different u and interesting
definitions to the eye when I'm looking at it from the and same way when we talk about inclusivity there's a
very interesting thing here the first definition says we are trying to look at development of algorithms and
techniques which mimic the way humans think right so when we look at that we are trying to understand
how humans think. A domain which philosophers and others had for a long time and now AI is also sort of
invading their domain so to speak or um adding to the domain taking something from the domain. So the
inclusivity here really is talking about how we are trying to understand ourselves, the way we think, the way
the brain functions and so on. There there's a an inclusivity. The other is we are looking at tasks which
traditionally were performed better by the human beings but now we are coming up with algorithms,
techniques, systems which do those tasks better than human beings. Face recognition is a classic example
today and many of the classification tasks today. We find that the AI systems outperform the human beings
on some of these tasks. Right now the inclusivity differs because when we look at this task, why did I have

this task? Why do I have facial recognition? What does it mean if I have an AI system which performs facial
recognition better than human beings today? Right? What is the target for that? And what do we mean by
inclusivity with respect to that? So I like to look at these things in a maybe grayish way rather than a binary
inclusivity is this inclusivity is not this. So um it is very very exciting time for us to be researchers in AI today
and many things are unanswered. Industry is driving many things forward sometimes to the point that a
faculty suddenly feels that uh am I being included in this industrydriven AI or am I being driven out of it
because of the simply the economics involved but that's not the way we have to look at this right so I like to
leave it open at this point and again I want to be on the fence on this at this point in time because as an
academician my job is to hold multiple viewpoints and not be forced to commit to one of them. I can hold
all of them in my brain at the same time and I don't become insane. That is the quality of an academician.

This panel discussion titled societal adoption of AI opportunities and challenges explores how collaboration
between technology developers, institutions and communities can help ensure AI adoption delivers
meaningful and equitable societal benefits. Our moderator for this panel discussion brings deep scholarly
insights into the fundamental and applied aspects of AI. Professor Vinit Balatyam is a principal researcher in
Microsoft research India as well as professor in computer science and engineering department at Indian

computer vision with a special focus onility, continual learning and learning with limited labeled data topics

next student speaker. Good morning respected judges, teachers and everyone present here. My name is
Gshri and I'm a grade 8 student from St. Peters High School here to share with you all the how artificial
intelligence quietly became a part of my life. Imagine sitting in front of a difficult math problem late at
night, feeling frustrated, confused, and desperate to find the answer. The books feel heavy. The clock is
ticking and doubt slowly begins to take over. Then with one question, one click, the solution appears. That is
all it takes for artificial intelligence to enter my life. What once felt impossible to complete suddenly feels
easy. What used to take hours now takes seconds. Artificial intelligence is no longer a future invention. It is
a powerful presence shaping my grades, my studies, and my daytoday life. As a grade 8 student, I see AI not
just as technology, but as a presence that changes how I learn, think, grow, and sometimes how much I rely
on myself. In studies, AI feels like a hero. When math formulas or science topics burden me, AI helps them
and explains it to me patiently without any judgment. When I was pressurized during my final exams, AI
helped me. It helped me revise faster. It explained concepts and it helped me overcome my fear of maths.
AI turned my doubt into clarity and my pressure into progress. But somewhere along the way, danger
creeps in. Outside textbooks, AI controls my life more than I realize. It reminds me when to do something. It
recommends me content. It answers almost everything instantly and simplifies nearly everything. This is
where the danger begins. When answers come without effort, our thinking ability slowly turns to dust.
When help is available more than necessary, struggle starts feeling unnecessary. Over time, discomfort can

turn into laziness. Even worse, constant screen time and addiction to AI can pull us away from real
conversations, real learning, and real creativity. AI is powerful but power without control does not protect it
destroys. That is why responsibility matters more than intelligence. AI always must remain a guide never a
replacement. It should ignite our curiosity not erased. Because real learning is born in confusion,
strengthened by failure and completed through effort, experiences no algorithm can feed, no machine can
replace, and no shortcut can ever teach us. In the end, AI is neither a hero nor a villain. It is a mirror. It does
not reflect its own intelligence but our choices. Our future is not shaped by how advanced machines
become but by how awake human minds remain. If used wisely, AI can uplift students like me higher than
ever before. If used blindly, it may begin to think in a place until one day we forget how to think at all.

Um, okay. So, um, let me just sort of uh, you know, it's a collection of slides just try try to illustrate these
things. So when you look at GPU utilization and I think uh it's like transformers 101 essentially you have
these layers of the transformer and then u there is uh you know computation that happens uh in the self
attention layer and then what's called the feed forward network but at a very high level when you ask chat
GP a query what you're giving as part of the prompt gets processed in parallel that's the blue stuff here right
so that's the query you're giving and then it produces the answer essentially token by token in an auto
aggressive manner right And so in some sense that's a sequential process. So what happens is that u the
blue stuff is uh pretty good at utilizing the flops of the GPU right essentially it is a computebound processing
but the token by token decode tends to be bottlenecked on the memory uh because you're producing one
token at a time and I think next slide probably just briefly illustrate that u and therefore you are idling your
GPUs and that from an efficiency viewpoint is not Um and uh the fact is that you know u compute operates
much faster than memory bandwidth and so that's the issue. Okay, this clicker is not very reliable. Okay, u
so we have done some work to uh essentially u make what is memory bound or to point that way. Okay,
thank you. But the machine is there. Okay, thank you. Okay, so um yeah, let me just jump forward. Right.
Yeah. So the other thing is okay this is like essentially looking at sort of one GPU but when you have uh first
of all I didn't mention this but today a lot of the cost and a lot of the energy is going in inference because
you know the models are trained and of course they are expensive to train but given that there are real
applications once you train it you're going to use it many many times right so inference dominates now um
when you're doing inference on a large model the model may be so large that it cannot fit in the memory of
a single GPU. Therefore, then the inference has to be spread across multiple GPUs, right? Whether in the
same node or across nodes and so the communication between these GPUs can also become a bottleneck.
So, from a systems viewpoint, those are things. So, um I think I'll just in the interest of time I see the
counter is either it's running very fast or I'm going slow. Uh so um essentially there's matrix multiplications
happening all over the place and in general the larger the matrix that you can multiply the more efficiencies
you get. So if you can accumulate a bunch of things and then do the multiplication that's better. So I'll leave
it at that. So what are the kinds of things that you can do and what we have done right on the left is the
picture today. you process the prompt in parallel and then you do this one at a time decoding for you know
possibly multiple requests and you're idling the GPUs instead you can take your prompt slice it up and pack
it with the decode we're calling it uh chunked prefills that's the idea right so essentially prefill is the parallel
initial part we chunk it up and we mix it up with the decodes in such a way that you can utilize this much
better so this idea of chunk prefill that we worked on maybe my colleagues worked on a couple of years ago
is now adopted widely right for example VLM is an open-source serving stack that came out of Berkeley I
think and you know it's it's part of that and it's part of other stacks as well so it's had quite a bit of influence
also published in a major conference um now that works for the so-called feed forward network but the
where you know everything is being multiplied by the same matrix so it's sort of easy but at the attention
layer it is hard to do this kind of chunking and repacking so where uh you know we can do sort of
parallelism at the level of these thread blocks or CTAs they're called where you take a streaming processor
and give it a combination of prefill thread blocks or CTAs and decode CTAs so that in combination the
compute and memory thing is balanced it utilizes both of them in a balanced manner right so at a very high
level that's the idea um so you can utilize both of these and finally when you get to this distributed
inference where the model is spread across multiple GPUs. Typically what happens is that you're processing
this batch, you're doing the compute and then you have to do a communication what's called all reduce
where the partial results from multiple GPUs have to be brought together and shared and so on right and
when that is happening you're idling your compute because the communication is happening. So again you
know from a systems viewpoint what you can do instead is you can split your batch into two and process
them in a staggered manner. So essentially while one of them is doing compute the other is doing
communication and you have to balance this. Of course there's a lot of intricacy here. So I'm just giving a
very high level picture and so you can avoid this u you know compute communication tension that exists. So
anyway this is a quick set of ideas that you can do. There are many other things that we have also worked
on and others are working on. So the second thing is offload, right? So I talked about using GPUs more
effectively. The second is offload. What does it take to leverage the GPU that's in your pocket in your phone

or in your laptop, right? Um you know there are many things but one thing that you have to worry about
and there's a lot of opportunity you know the GPU sitting idle might as well use it but you have to worry
about security right. So if you have a model you may not be comfortable shipping that model to an insecure
device which is the client device where you know the IP might get stolen right the the model is your IP and
you don't want that to be stolen and even if it is an open open weights model and you don't worry about IP
you have to worry about integrity how do you know that the client device is actually executing the model uh
that you intend to execute as opposed to doing something else So you have to worry about both model
protection and integrity, right? And so we have looked at uh both of these u um and the intuition for
example for uh for in the case of a model protection is the model may be very large but you can decompose
the weights into a relatively small critical part and a much larger non-critical part. Right? It's a very at a very
high level like 20,000 foot level. You can think of it as the most significant bits. A handful of most significant
bits if you hide the rest of the large number of less significant bits can't do much damage. Right? It's not as
it's not obviously uh it's a loose analogy. Uh so my colleagues have worked on this and you know they've
demonstrated that in fact this WC which is the critical part is quite tiny. So the idea is that you keep that in
the secure location like the cloud. You compute that part there. You do the bulk of the work on the
remaining bits on the insecure device. So you have shipped out bulk of the work, but you've made sure that
the important bits are still with you. And for integrity, I don't think I have time, but essentially again there's
some clever ideas leveraging [snorts] some work from 50 years ago uh by Frezols in 1977 to essentially
allow a probabilistic way of checking that the computation is actually uh accurate. So uh uh if there's
interest, I'm happy to uh uh tell you more offline or better still point you to my colleagues working on this
because they can answer this better than me. Um okay, so let me speed along. The third one I talked about
is small models. You know, you don't need the giant model for everything. And so I would say Microsoft
research globally, not just the India lab, has been among the pioneers in this idea of SLMs or small language
models where you're talking about models with maybe a few billion parameters. um that uh we're
essentially you know we are scaling or or we are pushing on the frontiers by working on the quality of the
training data right essentially curating the training data in a way that you can get great results for the task at
hand without having to uh you know scale the model right so we have this thing called the five series of
models and you know there's been a series of these models u and um and and that continues Now one
thing that we did in collaboration with our colleagues and some folks I mean in the India lab working with
uh the global set of labs is uh build a small language model for automating computer use right so think of AI
navigating a website selecting your flights and then booking the ticket for you you know it has to sort of
figure out how to navigate where to click or what to click what drop down to work on and so on right and so
we built this model uh I think yeah 7 billion So it's a fairly small model. Uh this was announced like 2 months
ago and got quite a bit of press. For example, uh in benchmarks we found that this rivals much larger
models like GPT40 and so on in terms of accuracy and that's what this chart also shows where you're
looking at accuracy versus cost. The far 7B is the leftmost curve here, right? So this just shows that you
know LLMs are great but they don't uh uh necessarily need to be the solution for everything. And finally
getting to power. Um we um uh you know obviously renewable energy is something that is growing
particularly in India. Um and um uh you know as AI energy consumption skyrockets. You have to think about
the consequences of that from a a sustainability sustainability viewpoint. So companies like Microsoft and
you know other large companies have made commitments on um you know being carbon neutral or carbon
negative in the next few years. So the question is [snorts] how do we get there? Now it turns out that the
renewable energy itself is not the bottleneck. The bottleneck is the transmission capacity. You might have a
you know a wind farm or a solar plant somewhere. getting power transmitted from there to where the
compute is is the bottleneck. So then the question is can we do this differently? So what my colleagues are
working on is this idea of green inferencing where instead of uh yeah instead of uh you know transmitting
the power to compute ship the compute to where the power is have micro data centers colloccated with
um these renewable energy plants and then figure out a clever way of shipping work to these right you

uh Google Deep Mind. Uh he's a PhD from the University of Texas Austin. He's widely known for his uh

Let's welcome Pratik. &gt;&gt; Thank you so much uh professor Nhari for the uh introduction and thanks to
Chiru and all the organizers for having me here at this wonderful event. Uh I must say that Chiru has put us
put me in a tough spot asking me to not only follow uh Shri Krish but also Wenard who is like you know an
inspirational research leader and I have had like you know fortune of learning from him for almost 10 11
years. Um so today I'm going to talk about um bringing adaptivity in uh large language models. So to sort of
you know set the stage, let's first uh look at how machine learning was done in say 2016 versus how it is
done in 2026. By the way, just in case you are thinking that I made this amazing slide, I did not. I just relied
on uh nano banana to sort of beautify my very sort of barebones slide. Uh anyway so in 2026 like the
approach was like while deep learning had become a commodity it has become very popular still the
general approach was let's take a problem let's figure out what are the accuracy requirements what are the
deployment constraints how much energy we want to spend in solving the problem how much uh like you
know latency we are willing to um have what kind of throughput or like queries per second we would want
to uh like address you take All of that you then collect some bit of uh label data and then you design a
neural network just for your task. You will say okay so for my current accuracy requirement for my latency
requirement for my power requirements I have this very specialized neural network that I train I will train it
I will validate it and then I will go and deploy it. Right? So that was the pipeline and while this pipeline was
successful still as you can see it had a lot of steps and you can get sort of you know bogged down by any of
the steps like like you know even defining many times the problem was hard collecting data training models
uh deploying models it was a challenge right life has changed significantly uh we are in 2026 where we are
in the generative AI era where all we need to do is think of a vague problem specification maybe cook up a
few examples give it to a large language models like say Gemini and that's it like the Gemini will start sort of
solving your problem in many many cases and you can just use the standard like the deployment framework
to go run your models so that's great so your time to sort of thinking about problem to going and actually
deploying it it has become like you know really really short but we are paying a price which is that we are
now relying on this one single foundational model to go solve our tasks, right? And this task might be very
different like this task might be super super complicated tasks but they can be very simple task like it could
be a task as simple as uh sentiment is it uh it might be as simple as a sentiment classification uh that okay
like you know I have this review of my product uh is the sentiment positive or negative for this product right
so because of that now what happens is one single model is serving these very different kind of tasks which
means that it will not be be able to sort of cater to the desired latency or cost or power requirements of
each of these tasks, right? Uh so the idea that we are bringing here is that can we bake adaptivity in the
model itself so that at test time so you can follow the same recipe you can still build one single foundational
model but within that model you have so much adaptivity baked in that you will pick up whatever required
part of the model for your individual task. So if your task is very simple you maybe pick a very very small
part of the model and just pass your data or query through it. But if the task is very challenging like solving
maybe an IMO problem, you would want to use then the full model in its full strength. All right. And to do
this like know we have come up with this sort of a general approach of matrioska models that is we would
want to put one model within the next model within next model. Um and this approach has sort of you
know we have tried it out in various ways. We have sort of you know uh made sure that like you know as
you sort of you know put this approach to test to on different axis on different sort of you know extreme
angles is it working or not and what we found is invariably so far like uh that the approach has been holding
you know along multiple axis. So I will not have time to talk about uh like you know all the work that we
have done in this space but let me give a very high level bit here. So the first line of work that we did was
around metuska embeddings or nested embeddings uh where the idea was that um when you are sort of
creating embedding of data let's say if I have a text document I can create an embedding of that document
which then I can use to retrieve the document or um or sort of you know find nearest neighbors to the
documents etc. So it's a very very common technique. Now in earlier times what used to happen was

people would come and say that okay I will create an embedding which is 256dimensional then somebody
will say no no no this 256 dimensional embedding of the document is not enough for me. I need much
smaller storage. So somebody will then have to design a new uh embedding model to create a 128
dimensional embedding. Somebody will to then redesign maybe 64 dimensional embedding. So you had to
redesign the same kind of architecture for multiple use cases. In metro embedding what we were able to do
was you train one single embedding model but within that single embedding model you can nest multiple
dimensional embedding. So you can within a 256dimensional embedding you can have a 128 dimensional
embedding you can have a 64 dimensional embedding and so on. So you train once, you release your
embeddings to your um clientele and then based on the requirements of each use case, people can pick and
choose how they want to go about um these embeddings. Right? So this approach became fairly popular
because of its generality. Uh it used it did not care for what was the underlying architecture, what was the
optimization function, what was the loss function, etc. It was a general technique. So it could be applied to
pretty much any setting. And uh because of that it has become fairly popular where pretty much all the uh
embedding models that are out there uh use this kind of nested structure. Last I checked on hugging face
like you know uh metric embeddings kind of models were downloaded maybe more 15 million times every
month etc. So it has been fairly sort of you know standard approach. Now the other then approach that we
came building upon this idea of metrica embeddings was that of matformermers that is we want to now
take our large language models and bring the same sort of uh nested structure within it. So there uh what
we did was in matformers we took one transformer architecture and we had this nested structure. In the
transformer architecture I'll talk about matforms in a little bit more detail. Uh time permitting I'll also talk
about matt quant where the idea is to have similar nested structure but in the quantization of model again
quantization becomes very very important to make sure that the model can be served at very low cost at
low power and in uh low latency regime. And finally we have um this nested routing kind of a structure
when we want to deal with very long context like if you have a large long video you want to understand
what is going on in the video. We have this uh new approach of Monet that can do this nested model
building to uh to make sure that we can do this long context video understanding in much much more costffective fashion. Let me quickly go over matforms. uh so like know this is your standard transformer
architecture when it also displayed it. Let's just focus on feed forward network for now because that's
where most of the cost and energy ends up going until you are looking at very long context uh inputs. So
there uh like the sweet for network has basically this very simple structure of like a key value retrieval that
is you get this token embedding. you look into this um like know database of W vectors and it tells you that
okay what all like know vectors might be important which is then denoted by this activation and then you
basically take those activations and you combine them in in some ways using this second matrix and you get
your next sort of token representation. So uh so this sort of you know part ends up costing the most in in
terms of power cost etc. So our initial thought was let's try to see if we can bring in the metroska
embedding structure within uh this feed forward network that is can I sort of say that I have these various
activations or or neurons I can sort of say that first 1/4 of the activations or neurons belong to my smallest
model and then the second set of uh activations that is let's say these activations belong to my
mediumsized model. These belong to my large-sized model and this belong to my full Excel sized model. So
within one model now I have nested my extra large, large, medium and small sized models right and like
you know now here we have we are using these exponential spaced granularities that is we are sort of
saying that okay if the largest model has say I don't know 16,000 activations then uh the large size model
should have say 8,000 activations and then 4,000 and 2,000 activations so that we are covering as wide a
range as possible. Okay. And now we can take this matformer block. We can put them together to form our
matformer model. Right. And while this idea I'm explaining mainly around the feed forward layer, but
actually this idea can be applied on pretty much all axis of your model. So it can be applied to depth, it can
be applied to attention, it could be applied to the the token representation of the token. uh so it can be it's
a fairly general idea and like know various uh follow-up works have also sort of taken it into various other
regimes like you know looking at it in the input regime output regime etc and um like know how do you
then go and train it so while I can define this architecture but I should be able to train this architecture in
efficient manner uh what we end up finding is that like the most simple thing that we could have thought of
works out fairly well that is if I take laws for each of these nested subm models add it up in some weighted
form and uh like you know create a joint training function and then let's just go and optimize it. Of course
you can sort of you know optimize it in ways so that it it doesn't end up costing you too much because one

option is that in every for every batch you will do forward and backward pass with every single model but
you can come up with more intelligent techniques like picking some of the model doing forward backward
pass only with them uh in certain batches. So those details aside uh the bottom line is that this can be
trained in an efficient manner and what we can now do get is in one single model we have these multiple
models nested right so that's good so here how many models will be nested there'll be like you know
basically uh three models nested within this one large excelized model so this can ensure that like know as
we are doing our adaptive inference we can pick and choose whatever models we want so let's say if I am
deploying my model on a mobile phone maybe I can pick Msize model and sorry on a mobile phone I can
pick my S-size model and deploy it there but on a more powerful laptop with GPUs uh powerful GPUs I can I
can put maybe a L-siz model so I can sort of you know pick and choose my models so that's good uh but the
interesting part is that now I can do something called mix and match to not only generate like these three
or four nested models but actually hundreds of nested models so the idea behind mix and match is that
within each layer I can pick one of the granularity. So let's say the bottommost transformer layer has Excel
sized granularity but the second layer has S-siz granularity and the third layer has maybe miz granularity. By
doing that you can actually pick hundreds or thousands or in fact like you know millions of models and now
what you can do is you can sort of figure out that what models performance is working best for your use
case for your uh power constraints for your data constraints right so this is what we end up finding so u due
to this flexibility we released metformermer in Gemma 3N which is uh the model that is powering Gemini
[clears throat] like the ondevice Gemini for Pixel 10 phones, various Samsung phones and various other um
partners uh Android partners and the advantage of Matformer here is that we have a two billion parameter
model sitting within the base 4 billion parameter model of Gemma. Uh which means that in the same RAM I
have not just a 4 billion parameter model but I have a 2 billion parameter model. In fact I have many more. I
have even a three billion parameter model, a 3.5 billion parameter model etc. All these models are sitting
there and now based on your application's requirement like know if your application is relatively easy like
say Gboard where you are just doing next word prediction you can use two billion parameter model but if
your application is a lot more complicated maybe you are using it for some sort of personal assistant you
would want to use full power of 4 billion parameter and here what we demonstrate is that uh as you
change the number of parameters the accuracy of the model changes smoothly that is this is our 2 billion
parameter model. This is our 4 billion parameter model. But these are the models that we just literally read
off by doing this mix and match. So we did not have to do any additional training, no additional distillation,
nothing. Just by reading it off, we were able to sort of generate this entire curve of cost versus accuracy or
number of parameters versus accuracy. Okay. All right. I have only 45 seconds remaining. So let me just give
a very brief overview of the next line of work that is which is that of metroska quantization. So idea here is
like you know even more simple when um as ward said that like you know when you are doing decode you
are mainly memory bound that is most of the time your GPU sitting idle uh all you are doing is you're taking
your weights from your high bandwidth memory and transferring it to the SRAMM on the device right and
uh so if you can cut down that cost then your latency reduces significantly for your decode actually it helps
like contization helps prefill also because you reduce the size of the model and in prefill you are very
constraint on the total uh memory that is available to you. So it helps both but mainly let's say decode. So
GMA 9 gemma let's say two models are like you know say int 8 quantized. So it's a very sort of standard uh
approach uh to make the model sufficient. But now if I have these 8 bit quantization model I can naturally
sort of say that why not nest multiple models within it. Right? that is I can put a 4bit quantized model and a
two- bit quantized model also that is I can what I can do is just take the first two bits here and that becomes
my two-bit quantized model and I can take first four bits here I can take create a four-bit quantized model
so the in some quantization already has this metric uh structure embedded within it uh although the issue is
that if you just create these two bit or four-bit quantization models because the models were not explicitly
trained with this kind of loss these kind of sort of you know quantization it ends up hurting the quality
significantly. So in um metroska quantization what we end up doing is uh so this is what this sliced thing
shows that is if you just slice out your intake model then the uh then the accuracy can decrease significantly
in metro share quantization what we do is we give a joint loss and directly optimize for that and if you do
that then what we observe is that the model's accuracy becomes significantly better in fact compared to say
training just a two-bit quantized model this metroshare quantization ends up giving you 3% lift at least on
some benchmarks. So it ends up in fact helping even your standard two bit quantization. But now the
advantage is that you can create the series of models also uh where the effective bit rate is not just two or

four or eight. You can also get a six- bit model. You can even get a three bit model, 2.5 bit model like
whatever effective um rate you want. And by using some more sort of tricks you are able to actually get this
entire parto curve to look very good like know to look almost as good as the best you could have hoped for.
Uh so skipping over all this uh like so one of the key idea that came in handy to make this parado curve
work is that of code distillation that is when we are training let's say into quantized model we are also
distilling from the intate quantization that we are running live. So by doing this live code distillation we
found that like the accuracy of the model can be improved significantly. Let me finish quickly. So basically
adaptivity and elasticity are like you know really really crucial components of AI models because as we go
towards like these single large giant models that are serving everything, we would want to make sure that
there are ways of using them in the way that best fits our application. And this principle of sort of you know
nested models or metroska generalizes fairly well across multiple sort of modern blocks and it is fairly easy
to use uh scalable. So I like you know please go try it. Okay. Thank you. &gt;&gt; Thank you. &gt;&gt; Thank

&gt;&gt; Our next speaker uh is professor Prashant Kumar Goss. He is on the faculty of electrical
engineering department at the Indian Institute of Science. Um he is a very accomplished researcher in

Um thank you professor Nari um for the nice introduction. Um thank you professor Chiru and the organizer
committee to invite me here. So for next few minutes um I'm going to talk about the journey of vani over
last three years. Uh it's a project um through which we wanted to capture the multimodal uh way of
capturing this Indian language landscape. So I'll talk about why what and how about vani. So let me begin
with why. Uh this is a cartoon way of capturing why we wanted to do vaning. Think of a lady from a remote
village in India trying to seek help of a service through her voice because she can't type. As you know many
of us use the voice feature of WhatsApp even don't type right some of them cannot even type and she finds
it difficult because the AI behind the service cannot recognize his uh her her voice and um so if we fail to
cover um you know users like her we fail to uh cover a majority of the in Indian population and mostly from
rural areas. So the idea was how do we really you know spread the uh solutions AI solutions today
particularly on voice uh to these people and population and also we know that India is not about one
language majority of the Indians can't speak English and often they speak multiple languages how we solve
all this problem uh is where Vani began just to give you an idea about some of the applications that are
voice enabled that exist way some of them are here on the screen and uh the biggest challenge that many
of them are not that widespread and successful is that India has multiple language of course you know
official languages or scheduled languages there are more non-scheduled there are more dialects and
accents across all those languages and how do you build solutions that cover all of them becomes a
daunting task. So if you look at u u how these models are built data is the uh gold for all of these models
and models build ornaments as I say so u what data exists today of course there's a constant effort of
building the data u I I'm talking about the voice data sets but as also may be aware of that it's
predominantly English and that makes uh it challenging to uh you know cater to some of the uh services
that that I just mentioned in the beginning. So if you look at the data sets in Indian languages um in this you
know bubble manner without running this is how it looks and we wanted to make a big impact on this
landscape. So one one of the reason that um we felt when we started three years back uh the limitations of
the existing approaches was that they were language centric approach. By that I mean when we collect data

we go to an individual and ask him or her to speak uh you know you know ask them to speak in Hindi or
speak in Telugu or speak in marati right but often we u did not realize that they may not be speaking the
marati we want them wanted them to speak they speaking something else and that's what we wanted to uh
discover through vani so this saying as you see on the top of the which says co bad pani jaros parvani it just
tells us that languages in India are not discrete they're like colors changing slowly from one to another over
the geography of the country and um we wanted to really quantify it discretetize it this continuous function
it was a challenge so what uh we realized that in India if you look at geographical there are 773 districts
right and how do we over the entire country was a challenge and we have to take a call. I'm just going to
come to that. So the philosophy of Vani based on these learnings was that we are going to go not in a
language anchored manner rather we're going to go in a district anchored manner. So it means we go to a
district and sample population some speakers and ask them what language do you speak at home? In fact
this level comes quite wrong. Some somebody thinks that I they speak Hindi or he or she speaks Hindi but
actually they're speaking. So that becomes but it also helps us discover languages which are even not
captured in the latest census. So that's first one and because it's continuous it helped us to capture this
slight variation in languages. So the vision of Vani is to create this language dialectri corpus build solutions
on top of it to discover whether the existing tools solutions uh already are good or they they need still
improvement and um of course when we build these tools we along with we do the empowering of
stakeholders and democratize uh AI for all these India languages. So let me move on and let me tell you why
we wanted to take this approach. So in Vani uh what we do we present an image to a speaker and ask that
speaker to talk about that image. So this helps us to solve many problems. Number one the speaker need
not be literate. Number two, the image that are captured, they are local that from that district. So that
naturally captured the diversity and culturally uh you know unbiased way of representing the Indian
continent. And third it increases the vocabularies because as we see many many images it also brings lot of
associated words as these people speak right and people also found it very you know comfortable to talk
about an image at their home in isolation not being confronted by or you know by another speaker and
they may feel shy to So traditional approaches often use u red speech or conversational speech but we we
chose image prompting for all those reasons. So as of today we are at the end of phase two of Vani. We
have now open sourced 31,000 hour plus speech from 156,000 speakers from 165 districts having a
coverage of 109 languages speaker declared out of those 2,000 plus hours transcribed. So that's where we
stand today and we'd like to take it forward. So this is a visual way of presenting all the statistics. I'll not
repeat that. Let me move on to the core u part of it. Uh so here here are some samples. Um maybe I'll not
go into playing u uh for now but this just represent uh if if you go to van web page we do present uh a way
where you can go to a district go to a you know sample speaker an image and can listen to that person's
voice is truly represent this language diversity. So there is a complex process behind this outcome and this
slide captures that u in brief. So one is a multimodal data. It has images. It has audio recordings. It has
transcription. Right? For each of them we had to follow a very detailed set of steps to make sure the
outcome is of high quality. Still we have challenges. So for image uh it's captured by the local district people.
We have to make sure the images are proper. It should not contain PII which is called personal identifiable
information. Right? and it also has diversity. Then for the audio, we get the recordings and it goes through
multiple levels of automatic, semi-automatic and manual checks before it is released in public. And same for
transcription. um a person types out by listening what he or she hears and it goes through a total of three
validation stages to make sure that the outcome the what is typed is actually correct and I'll come to some
learnings that happened because of that. So in this journey we also collect from every speaker a very rich
metadata starting from not just their gender and age but also their socioeconomic status to understand the
correlation between what kind of background they are from what is their status and the speech if there is
any and many others. So here are some samples. So for example, if you look at uh this map, it shows per

location is uh not reachable by transport or even because of due due to lack of internet and so on so forth

has become very very difficult in some of the districts. Then the next was getting skilled people. You may

coming to the transcription part in a bit. Uh so getting skilled people was quite difficult because it requires

over the years is a challenge. Let me tell you some of the learnings and that's the one I was mentioning
couple of times which is in the top left about high W difficulty. So W stands forward error rate. So if you get
a recording and give it to two individuals from the same district where the recording was done, we realize
that those two people's transcription or they type do not match and they don't match because of many
many reasons. And when you calculate how different they are through something called word error rate,
how many words do not match, we realize that that rarely go beyond 10%. So there is a inherent
disagreement between multiple transcribers uh as we get it done even through the native person of the
respective districts. And what we realize also is that if you give that to a regular transcriber of that language
then it goes even more than that 30 40% of the year. So let me give you one example. If you take Telugu as
a language we have found four major variants. One is Guru Krishna another is Vishakapatnam area another

&gt;&gt; Yeah, I I'm okay. Can I have the first slide? &gt;&gt; Okay. Thank you so much for the opportunity.
uh if there is one industry which will get completely disrupted beyond recognition with the AI is going to be
healthcare industry. In the next few minutes I'm going to talk about what AI can do in healthcare. not about
a hypothesis, something happening in US or Europe, something happening right inside my hospital. We
started a AI division around 9 years ago and most people started talking about AI only after chat GPT and
we were using AI for many aspect of healthcare at least more than five to six years. I I believe that India will
become the first country in the world to dissociate healthcare from wealth. India will prove to the world
that the wealth of the nation or wealth of the family has nothing to do with the quality of health care its
citizens will enjoy. And many of you may think yeah it's going to happen but it may happen after 30 years or
100 years. It's going to happen in the next five to seven years because most of the foundation for this
massive transformation is already available. It is just there and someone has to put all the pieces together
and that is happening very fast. I'll just give you one example of how we use AI. This slide what you're
seeing is the blood gas report. It is the one of the tests which is commonly performed in the ICU to check
the status of the patient and the report comes with about 20 25 different data points. ICU has many people
working but very very few handful of doctors and nurses can decipher the data then plan the treatment and
just below the blood gas report you can see the AI interpretation of the blood gas report and also it not only
interprets it also advises the doctors and nurses what to do Now before this pilot started very few nurses
knew how to interpret blood gases. Just one month after we started virtually all the nurses in the ICU have
become experts in uh interpreting and taking action on the blood gas reports. This is the power of AI. It is
not only smart, it makes everyone using it very very smart. This is one example. The next example is I'll this
is called risk score. A patient after the heart operation stays in the ICU for 24 hours or few days and there
are multiple data points which will either go towards improvement or deterioration and these are all very
subtle uh signs and symptoms and it is very difficult for a doctor to make any sense looking at one or two
signs and one or one or two signals. Our AI tool interprets about 70 odd data points and 6 hours to 10 hours
before the final cardiac arrest. All these data points will keep moving up and down. So you can actually
predict a so-called sudden cardiac arrest at least 5 to 10 hours before the event. This is the beauty of uh all
these technology tools. So what is the advantage? All over the world there is shortage of intensivist. India is
no exception. If a intensivist is working inside the hospital then they work for 8 hour shift. The best ICU care
patient gets between 9:00 in the morning and 5:00 in the evening. After 5:00 till next day morning 9:00
patients are managed by relatively junior nurses and relatively junior intensist and if you look at the safety
patient is the safest between 9 to5 that shouldn't happen that should change how do you change it we tell
the intensivist that at night you don't need to be in the hospital you can be anywhere where you want but 4
hours of the 16 hour cycle you take the on call and in their laptop they can see every patient all the events
happening they can interact with the nurses and the uh the doctors who are based there and the moment
the risk score starts going up they will know what exactly has changed and they will uh the the software
itself will suggest what needs to be done but ultimately it's the doctor who has to take the final decision and
when this transformation happens in a large scale hospital will become safer for the patients. Why everyone
thinks that the hospitals are the safe places for the patients? Unfortunately, it is not. Let's look at some of
the safest hospitals on the planet in US. If 200 patients spend 24 hours in a hospital, one in 200 dies due to
medical error, not medical negligence. And according to joint commission of US, the commonest cause of
medical error 67% is due to communication failure. that is something is happening but the decision making
people don't get that information the way it should be and all these tools will help the doctors to take the
action well before the final cardiac arrest happens. This is the power of AI tools which will empower the
doctors. It will make the doctors safer for the patients. So the essentially in a matter of time you will have
intensive care units without intensivist. They will all be working from remotely and they cover only for 4
hours or 5 hours at night but in taking in rotation. So every doctor whenever he's taking care of the patient
he is fresh after his sleep or before the sleep. The one of the nightmare for a hospital is discharging the
patient in the morning. When your occupancy is about 80%, there is no bed available. Morning you have to
admit at least 60 patients and they come there and you need to discharge 60 patients on a before we
implemented AI it used to take us over five hours to discharge a patient. If you have ever admitted one of
your patients in the hospital, you know the problem of discharging a patient and the b biggest problem is

creating the medical record and creating a discharge summary. We had 30 girls running around like
headless chickens early morning trying to discharge patients on time. And now our AI tool in the in our ADI
which is the tool we have in as a EMR you have to just tap one button say discharge the patient entire
discharge summary gets generated it comes like this it is autopop populated and a doctor who is on his way
to the hospital sitting in his car he can check all the data and advise for discharge or make any changes
entire process can be done within 5 to 10 minutes. So this is the it is not a transformation of efficiency. It is
a different experience altogether. In a heart hospital there are not more than 30 different types of
operations we do. It's the same operation. We keep doing it. And what is different with each patient is the
complexity or so-called findings. So when the doctor finishes the operations uh cardiac surgery straight
away after few minutes he has to go to the another operation theater and one of the hardest thing for a
hospital administrator is to uh get the doctors to write the operation notes and normally it takes about at
least 15 to 20 minutes to complete one operation notes and when they skip from one OT to the they forget
the major finding in the previous uh patient and you may not get a very accurate operation notes. Now
what they do when they finish the surgery all they have to do is to find what is different in this patient. They
only talk to the AI what is different and everything else is standard and within 3 to four minutes the AI will
generate the operation notes. He can correct it in his own phone and it will be entered into the system. One
of the biggest change we made in electronic medical records is that we decided to build the entire
electronic medical records uh on a mobile phone with the mobile first concept whereas all over the world
EMRs are developed for the desktop. Why we made the change? Because doctors look at the laptop or a
desktop five to six times in a day but they look at the mobile phone 200 times in a day. So if you want their
attention create the EMR for the mobile phone. This is what we did and that's what brought in significant
uh efficiency all over the world. When a doctor is doing the echo cardiogram or ultrasound there are scribes
sitting next to them and typically doctor does the echo and they keep dictating when he start dictating
patient will think that he's talking to him so he will try to respond then as and when as they are doing the
echo in a random fashion they can keep dictating in five minutes or 10 minutes or 15 minutes when they
finish the echo all they have to do is to tap the enter button entire echo report gets generated and it comes
in this format then they can correct it if there is a mistake and they one finally say it's enter it will go to the
system and the report will appear in the patient's mobile phone before they can put on their shirt after the
echo cardiogram this experience is nothing short of magic So these are the experiences which makes entire
consumer experience. We don't call the patient as a patient. We call them as a consumer because the the
the experience of the patient should be like a consumer who goes for consuming some services. So the
consumer experience will be dramatically different. We are in the process of building about four or five
hospitals which will be commissioned in the next five months to maybe one or one year and all those
hospitals will have people in the scrubs only working inside the building. People who are not going to touch
the patients, people who are not wearing the scrubs, they don't need to work in the hospital. They can work
remotely and support the hospital. So they can support multiple hospitals than one hospital. So that will
dramatically decongest the hospital and brings in huge efficiency. So the when you enter our hospitals
across the country you will find the reception without the receptionist. There are kiosk everywhere and
believe me we think that the patients are not techsavvy but I with our experience we have noticed they are
much much smarter than we are the they can in no time they learned how to use the kiosk and today a lot
of them do the registration and pay the money in their own mobile phone they don't even go to the kiosk
the when we first introduced this mobile registration with our app. they could register, they could pay the
money and one particular patient told one of our doctors that sir I have paid the registration fees but your
security hasn't deposited the money and the doctor asked him how do you know he hasn't deposited then
he opened the NHare app and showed the doctor that it is not entered the the the payment is still pending
whereas I have paid the money and we realize that He's right. The secretary did pocketed the money and he
could pick it up when we missed it. So the essentially our assumption that this technology is not suitable for
our rural India, it is not true because most of our customers come from rural background. So the one of the
apprehension everyone has in healthcare is that whether AI will replace the doctors. This is the statement
by Dr. Curtis Langlords from Stanford. AI won't replace the radiologist. He's a radiologist himself. He said AI
won't replace the radiologist but the radiologist who uses AI will replace those who don't. So the there is no
threat for anyone that they are going to lose a job in healthcare. It is just that the way they function will be
different and in the end all these technologies will make health care safer for the patient. Now we believe
when I talk about dramatic transformation uh in healthcare this happens because of digital massive digital

adoption. I just want to give you the example of world's largest taxi company called Uber has no vehicles.

right so so what are we doing slightly differently in terms of healthcare technologies when it comes to AI for
health right so the fundamental uh direction of protocols design of studies of identifying problems to work
with we take an AI first approach which essentially means to say that when you collect the data, when you
get the first piece of information about the patient and so forth, uh all the way up to treatment or
suggestions, advice, counseling, etc. We want to ensure that these are all digitized, these are all consumable
by modern AI technologies and therefore that becomes core to various technology development. And then

the other aspect is not just on the uh research and development but also on the deployment of these

approvals in the country to get this as SMDs which essentially means software as a medical device safety
and ethics. So this is significantly important because uh healthcare is personal. It is also individual. It also
has lots of uh other uh domains that AI typically doesn't address in terms of fairness, inclusivity, in terms of
somebody mentioned about explanability and so forth. So all of these aspects somehow have to come in
play when AI becomes uh uh part of the healthare system. So then uh just very quickly going straight at
where we try to focus. So we call ourselves the tanu which stands for translational AI for network universal
healthcare. And if you look at the continuum of care cycle that individuals go through when they identify
themselves as uh patients or persons at risk is that they try to seek care and then they go through the
current arrival at the first point of care. This could be your health workers, nurses, Asha workers in a semi
semi- urban or rural settings and then some diagnosis is done and then they are typically pushed into the
subsequent chain of the healthcare which involves treatment of uh the disease or definitive diagnosis
through other sophisticated tools etc. So when we proposed the AI solutions we said that we want to work
at the first point of care. This is where the widest margin of uh population comes in. This is where also there
is the significant source of error right so typically the non-fysician health workers the nurses the asha
workers and so forth they generally tend to lack the medical or clinical knowledge that is needed and they
also have significant resource constraints like you know you don't have definitive diagnostic tools you don't
have uh ultrasound and uh other sophisticated scanning devices and they still have to make certain
decisions about the person that is coming in to seek the care. So this is where we felt that you know the
knowledge of having more data which can be summarized through AI technologies this is where they can
play a role to improve the efficiency of the system. Right? So this is fundamentally where many of the
healthcare solutions that we are trying to operate on come from. So again in terms of the various uh topics
that are of interest in tanu these are primarily in uh oral cancer led by professor Rajeshan here in ECE.

meaning they have to become an app. It sits resides in the uh cell phone the smartphone or the health

developing these AI models it also has to walk through all the paths of typical healthcare technology which
means to say that all of them have to go through ethics and approval of the ethical committee scientific

basically take it and treat it as a classification problem. And then you know the question that I particularly
liked at the end of the previous talk was uh the comment that you know there is the person coming with
the cancerous sign and the and the model misses it or the vice versa the model comes up with a false
positive both are equally bad. So what we have been trying to also do in my lab and which also we want to
extend to some of the solutions we provide to the AI center of excellence is to have models to in addition
providing classification outputs generate confidence estimates generate trustworthy responses meaning to
say that okay I am feeling that this individual scan is cancerous but this is the confidence I have in it and
now you pass on both the decision and the confidence to the human in the loop to consider when they
make the decisions. So how we why we need all this is because there is insufficient data, mismatch data,
there is noisy data, medical domain. You never really have complete diagnosis at any point in time. You

always want more data from the patient but at the same time we were training and testing maybe
mismatch which means that you have situations where data is also going to be uh out of domain for the
models you have trained. So in these settings sometimes model even models even exhibit hallucinatory
behavior. Sometimes they are going to perform uh very poorly in certain conditions. So now can the
confidence estimate be a reliable metric that you output that can be taken into the decision process. So I'm
not going to go into the details of the machine learning aspects that have helped us do this but primarily to
say that with more sampling with more inferences with more analysis with the model one can come up with
some uncertainty metrics which essentially the whole scheme of things help models to also say what we
commonly do as humans if you take let us say a nurse if you take even doctors with certain medical data
points they commonly say I don't know we need more tests right now models also need to have this that if
there is insufficient evidence to decide in one way or the other we want to enable models to say I don't
know right so now you can pass it on back to the human and then therefore back to the chain to do more
definitive diagnosis to pick up that threat and then further probe to get a definitive response right so

inviting me and all the organizers for putting this together. Um, I'll start with I think a widely accepted idea
is that the primary medium of interaction between man and machine is going to be voice. I think today the
way we interact with the computer primarily is either through touch or through type and over time if you
see history and how humans have interacted with a machine it has evolved. I think if you've seen the Steve
Jobs versus Bill Gates YouTube documentary there's this part where Bill Gates is putting punch cards into a
machine to code it. And I think over time if we see it went from the punch cards to typing in during the time
of MS DOS then graphic user interface came in with Macintosh and Xerox and the iPhone brought touch at
least the multi-touch screen came to all of us. I think if you see when this happened every time this change
happened where the primary medium of interaction between man and machine changed it unlocked
massive value. So if you see the number of companies that got built with every single change the number of
internet users or people who got access to the computers drastically increased. So if we see this movement
has been towards being something that's more intuitive to humans and I think voice is the most intuitive
way for humans to interact with each other even much more than touch and once that happens I think it
will unlock so many possibilities that we don't imagine today for example when touch happened we
couldn't imagine that probably something like an Uber will now exist but it happened because the iPhone
happened. So I think the change to voice when the primary medium of interaction between the machine
and humans becomes voice it will lead to a mindblowing impact in the new kinds of tools and services that
get built. So at Jotos we are incredibly excited about contributing to making this change happen. I think
there's something even more intuitive than voice and that is maybe if neural link works and we can just
think and it happens that would be super cool but I think for now we'll stick to the voice a bit. So we did
something interesting um it is then the the narrative around voice AI is that it's become really good and at
least it's usable and there are so many tools and services around us. But if I really ask any of you today since
the morning, it's around 4:00 now, 3:30 now. How many of us have actually significantly used a voice agent
since the morning? Any of us have had very significant usage with a voice agent since morning. I think that's
maybe like one person, right? That's like one person out of this audience of maybe 150 to 200 people.
That's staggering. There is something going on that is not working. I'll give you another uh idea here. The
mean opinion scores or the MOS scores of the latest TTS models show that the MOS is at 4.6 PE models are
claiming 4.6 4.7 MOS on a scale of five. Then where is the usage? There is something that is broken in the

middle. So what we did is this is not a research grade study. This was an internal study we did to get a sense
on this feeling that we have that voice AI is broken. Can we get a sense on what do users actually think? So
we went to about a 100 users who have nothing to do with technology who are who don't create products
who are just the consumers of the internet and of computers. And we asked them to rate that if the
interaction that you have with your mother or your father is five on five human on that scale where do
voice agents today stand. So some of these people are the users of things like Alexa and Siri but they're also
the users of the chat GP voice mode and the Grock voice mode and so many other voice agents that we
have access to today. And it's staggering that the average score that we got is a two on five. people feel that

taking place within the framework of the AI impact summit's working group on resilience innovation and
efficiency. Now uh conventional AI development as we understand it has become a very resource inensive
and expensive proposition. Large AI models need enormous amounts of computational power, vast data
sets and uh they are massive energy guzzlers as well. And just to give you an idea of the scale of what we
are talking about, it cost a hundred million US simply to train Chat GPD4 which many of us use and the
model was trained on some 13 trillion tokens of data. Uh the power needed by advanced AI models is is
doubling roughly every 100 days driving up the demand for electricity and water. A typical AI focused data
center today consumes about as much uh electricity [clears throat] as 100,000 households and uh you know
some of the latest ones which are currently under construction consume 20 times as much. All right so
these are staggering figures and uh being water guzzlers as well um this is proving really quite detrimental
to water scarce in arid regions. All right, many of which happen to be in the global south. U so we can see
right away that there's a convergence of economic, environmental and accessibility hurdles when it comes
to building AI at scale. High resource requirements often put the creation and use of AI beyond the reach of
most economies especially those in the global south and developing countries which also means that uh
many of those who could have benefited most from AI tend to be excluded and AI's resource intensity is
also why less than one in five companies manages to scale AI successfully and sustainably. So the big
questions before us today are how do we integrate efficiency into AI development? How do we make AI not
just more affordable and accessible but somewhat more responsible as well and what sorts of policy

largest models will train with like really really large amount of data that then helps ensure that most of the

Mr. Rabishek Aarwal to address the gathering. Okay. Thank you. So as we conclude this session, I would like
to thank all our speakers and participants for a very focused and constructive discussion. Today's exchanges
clearly highlighted that the future of AI will not depend only on innovation but on how efficiently and
responsibly AI systems are designed and scaled. As adoption accelerates, issues of energy usage,
infrastructure stress, and system reliability are no longer secondary concerns. They are central to long-term
process. A key takeaway from today is that efficiency must be built on into AI systems from the outset. This
is not just a technical choice but a strategic one with implications for sustainability, accessibility, and
resilience. We also heard the importance of designing AI that can operate reliably reliably under the real
world constraints and remain robust in the face of disruption. Equally strong message was on collaboration.
[snorts] No single country or institution can address these challenges alone. Shared learning and
international cooperation will be critical in shaping practical and adaptable pathways for resilient and
resource conscious AI. As we move towards the India AI impact summit 2026, the insights of this working
group will directly inform our work on guiding principles and pract practical pathways for efficient AI. These
conversations will help ensure ensure that global ambition is grounded in real world realities. I warmly
invite all of all of you to continue this engagement and join us at the India impact summit 2026 where these
conversations will come together at a global level. Thank you once again for your time, your insights and
your continued engagement. We look forward to carrying this work work together. Thank you. &gt;&gt; I
