SPEAKER / SESSION: Dr. Romesh Wadhwani on ‘Exploring Global AI Policy Priorities Ahead of the India AI
Impact Summit’ [TWAJSTAlRvY].en
====================================
and welcome to all our guests and looking forward to a great conversation this morning. Uh just a little
aside, uh as John was saying, uh I've been in AI for more than 50 years. uh before people knew that AI was
going to be a thing uh or from the time I went to Carnegie Melon University, the first two professors of AI
were there. So I was fortunate to learn from them and then building my multiple companies over the long
period of time in many of them I have used that then I would say super primitive versions of AI. So of course
when uh today's AI technology started to emerge you know just a few years ago I was uh perhaps among
the most excited of the participants in this uh great journey that we've been on but I think uh all of you
know that AI is going through explosive growth uh you may not know the degree of explosiveness, if I can
use that word, that we're about to see in the next five years. I'll talk about that. But I think most of all when
you see this kind of disruptive technology change compressed into just a 5-year time frame as compared to
previous technology revolutions that took 20, 30 or 50 years. Uh the impact on society, on business, on
government is unprecedented. It's not something that we have seen before. And of course it calls for a
strategic policy response. So I thought this morning I would focus on sharing some thoughts on that kind of

strategic policy response from four different powers. The USA, China, Europe and India. U perhaps some of
the things I have to say are just you know personal impressions and personal uh sort of forecasts. uh some
you know everyone some of the some of the thoughts I have everyone may not agree with but I thought
they are worth sharing so we can have a discussion and hopefully uh drive strategic policy forward uh next
slide uh so you use chat GPT I'm assuming that &gt;&gt; recording in you in the audience &gt;&gt; but I
must tell you that what was breakthrough technology 3 years ago now seems kind of quaint. Uh just three
years ago GPT became part of our vocabulary. Billions are using it. But the world is now shifting to a world
of AI agents and because that word AI agent is much abused. Uh people refer to any piece of code that has
some connection with AI as an agent. I wanted to define uh a full function AI agent as something that is a
software entity that knows its context, reasons, plans, executes, learns, and improves. Now, you're probably
telling yourself, well, that sounds pretty much something like a human being. And the answer is yes. There
are obviously very large similarities between what an AI agent can do and what a human being can do. And
a full function AI agent can augment, replace or surpass human workers. And we will be in this journey from
augmentation to surpassing over the next 5 years. And I'll talk about that next slide. The era of AI agents has
arrived and my projections are that last year was 2025 there were less than 5 million AI agents and their
role was primarily to assist and augment human beings and year by year over the next 5 years we will see
200% or greater compound annual growth in the number of AI agents and the capabilities of AI agents. So
as you see in this slide, AI agents this year will begin to collaborate and execute workflows autonomously,
meaning no human intervention required. Then they will start replacing humans in many tasks. Then
probably in two or three years there will be the emergence of artificial general intelligence for the
enterprise. These AI agents will then start operating major processes autonomously and five years out
somewhere between 1 billion and 5 billion artificial general intelligence AGI agents will be able to operate
the entire business enterprise autonomously possible exception of the board of directors, the CEO and
obviously a certain you know important group of senior managers to keep everything ship shaped. So that's
a pretty remarkable journey and it would be one thing for me to tell you this is a you know future vision 50
years out but this is a future vision 5 years out. Next slide. Now in this context AI policy will determine
winners and losers. So AI technology superiority will determine winners in geopolitics and national security.
National adoption and a commitment to that will determine winners in economic and GDP growth. Massive
deployment by enterprises will determine winners in business and exports. Regulation will determine the
winners in scale and velocity of innovation. i.e. less regulation equals faster innovation and vice versa. and
reskilling and support for AI displaced workers will determine the winners in social stability. So you can see
that there are at least five major dimensions of policy that need to be addressed and different geopolitical
powers are handling this in different ways. Next slide. So these are the four powers we'll talk about US,
China, Europe and India. Next slide. So I think of the US uh with its focus on global dominance and AI
technology as the land of the free. You all know that this is you know part of our American credo. We are
the land of the free but we will be the land of the free for AI agents and not just for humans. So in the US AI
sits at the center of national security and geopolitics. Uh there are certainly some export controls which are
changing and potentially there will be a requirement to register and report the performance of frontier
models. But at least in the current era regulation is light touch and there will be federal dominance of AI law
and preeemption of state laws. So this is current US policy. Next slide. In Europe, I think of it at least in
terms of AI as the land of regulation before innovation. So the AI act was uh passed a year more than a year
ago but its provisions kick in August of this year and they have a very high regulatory burden uh which will
certainly have some benefits for the citizens of Europe but will have the disadvantage of continuing to keep
innovation at a much lower velocity than in other geographies. Uh this law for example includes frontier
model oversight uh audits, incident reporting, risk mitigation, uh emphasis on consumer protection over
industrial innovation and failure to comply can lead to huge fines uh as much as 7% of an enterprises
revenue. Next slide. China's focus is on tech competitiveness and political control. So I think of China in AI as
the land of political control and mandatory adoption. Because of that, it will have the largest global
adoption of AI agents in every sector of business and government because they simply won't have a choice.
It'll be mandated. But in China, everything has to be kept under political control and aligned with the
priorities of the CCP. Uh China will be uh in a continuing uh battle or horse race with the US uh for
competitiveness and co-leership in AI technology. And there is a high probability that using AI to a greater
and greater degree, China will leaprog all other countries in productivity and competitiveness in spite of the
fact that it will have an extensive regulatory framework. Many elements of that framework are already in

place. I have no doubt that more and more regulations will be added as the uh number and capabilities of AI
agents continues to proliferate. Next slide. India's focus with AI is around economic growth. So I think of
India as the land of practical innovation using AI. I think there will be massive national scale deployment of
AI in government and enterprise. Uh very high focus on innovation and reskilling and combined with I would
say relatively light regulation. Uh there aren't any current major laws that slow down the practical
innovation with AI. There could be some limited laws in the future and uh there is a a pretty strong data
protection law in India but best as I can tell it's not slowing down the uh adoption of AI in India. Next slide.
India's strategy and again I want to be clear this is my interpretation. It's not that the Indian government has
issued a strategy document that I am reading from. I I just look at all the things that are happening in the
Indian context in AI and these are the seven uh aspects of India's AI strategy. First is become one of the top
three in AI after US and China. the uh a lot of the uh emphasis of the Indian government is about making
sure that they don't lose this moment. Uh interesting side note here as John mentioned uh the Vadwani
Foundation's mission uh is to accelerate job creation in India and many other uh emerging geographies
emerging countries through large scale initiatives in entrepreneurship in skilling and in innovation. And in
2018 which is now you know 6 years ago uh Vadwani Foundation uh launched uh the Vadwani Institute uh
for AI for social good as a philanthropic initiative and I invited the prime minister to come and inaugurate
that institute. He was very generous and flew from Delhi to Bombay, Mumbai in February of 2018 again 6
years ago to inaugurate the institute and I I still haven't forgotten. He gave one of the most enlightened
speeches about how AI could be used for the good of India and for the good of the world. And he said in the
end uh while AI will cause disruption again this goes back six years uh ultimately the impact of AI whether
for good or for bad will come down to human intent. And he said if the human intent is good then all will be
good with AI. And if human intent is bad, then I guess problems will emerge. So here we are 6 years later
and those those words were obviously prophetic. Uh but he's been very engaged with AI ever since then
and certainly it's translated into many initiatives that have been launched uh and the desire for India to be
in the top three. Uh number two, I think India will become a leading innovator in AI applications and not in
capital inensive frontier models or LLMs. the gigantic investments required to be a competitive player in
building a frontier model or an LLM when the world already has half a dozen players who have built these
uh core LLMs. Uh seems like there's a better way that uh India's strategy can evolve. uh certainly I think
India is going to have a sovereign LLM to support multiple languages and to become part of India's
sovereign AI infrastructure but a lot of the LLM technology that India will use uh will in fact be based on
technologies that have already been developed over the last few years. Third strategy is massive
deployment of AI in enterprise and government. And in fact the AI economic summit that is about to begin
in the next couple of weeks uh will focus and in fact if you look at the agenda topics uh for that summit uh
very strong focus on uh the use of AI in enterprise and government. Number four, I talked about uh India
building a sovereign LLM or multiple of them uh in multiple languages. Uh number five, I think India can and
will become a leader in domain specific reasoning models applied to specific needs in government or
business or consumer. uh India does have a very strong commitment to transforming the workforce for AI
readiness through massive reskilling and as I mentioned light regulatory touch. So basically India's strategy
is to become the go-to AI geopolitical and applications leader for other emerging countries. Next slide.
These are India's key initiatives. I'm not going to go into details on each and every one of them but India
launched the AI mission back in 2024 basically to democratize access to AI and build sovereign AI
infrastructure. India launched a compute infrastructure initiative as a public private partnership again to
strengthen uh AI infrastructure and accelerate startups and enterprises academia and government in using
AI. Uh India has launched a number of workforce AI readiness programs lot more to do by the way because
the impact as you'll see in the next few slides is going to be pretty enormous. uh well begun a lot more to
do multiple national initiatives in healthcare in agriculture in education in citizen services again I'm

trillion dollars starting at about a hundred billion dollars of incremental GDP in 2026 and climbing yearbyear
to 500 billion in 2030. So looking at India's GDP in 2030, if the baseline was going to be 6.5 trillion, nothing
to do with AI, it has the potential to be around $8 trillion, 1.5 trillion more than the baseline. Now what's
the job market impact? Again, best guess, my guess, over the next 5 years, cumulative job growth due to AI.
40 million plus new kinds of jobs created with AI. Unfortunately, offsetting that will be 20 million job losses
in IT, in BO, in knowledge workers and enterprises, in particularly entry- levelvel jobs. there will be a lot of
job dislocation. So the good news is compared to many other geographies there will be net job gains and
not net job losses but the net job gains won't be as big as the full potential that AI provided because it will
be offset by dislocations also caused by AI. So round numbers personal guess only 20 million plus net new
jobs created due to AI sitting on top of India's baseline uh you know uh job uh sort of uh foundation. Uh

technological opportunities and potential. What are the key ethical um or procedural let's say safeguards
that you think India's courts should prioritize when engaging with AI systems especially given the diversity
and scale of the justice ecosystem. &gt;&gt; Um thanks for that question and thanks for organizing this and
having me here today. Um you know if you look at AI integration into the judiciary you can broadly say
there are two areas it can be integrated. one is in sort of helping streamline administrative load of courts.
So whether that'suling of cases um um or it can be used in more substantive matters like research,
translation of documents um or then you know even decision making and we've seen a spectrum of
applications being developed that that help across uh the cross-section of these tasks. Um some have
higher risk uh such as decision making. Um some have sort of uh downstream impacts on significant
decision- making like if you're translating documents and if there errors over there. Um and then of course
um it may seem that administrative decision-m being taken over by AI um may not impact justice but
actually even use of AI anduling can impact justice because AI deciding which case gets listed first which

case is extraneous um can sort of uh really impact someone's timelines for justice delivery. So I think you
know we need to of course categorize AI use in judiciary across various risk categories and then for each
category we need to think about what is the level of assessment that needs to be undertaken. Um you
know there are various risk assessment auditing mechanisms that are being established in the AI domain.
uh and you know there needs to be thought about how those can be translated and used in the judicial
context. So I think that's sort of one piece is auditing and assessments for questions of bias for questions of
transparency for questions of explanability. Um the second piece that I think we need to be thinking about
is judiciary sits typically in any country on on a large trove of data. I know within the Indian judicial
ecosystem uh as part of the e-comittee process the Supreme Court was really thinking about how do you
unlock the value of that data and use it to streamline court processes. How can you give it to stakeholders
in the ecosystem whether it's startups, other public interest stakeholders, researchers to sort of go through
that data and figure out okay you know this these are the lessons and this is how we can streamline
processes to move through case load quickly. But that also means that you need to have very robust data
management practices uh you know data protection principles being applied because this can in many
instances be sensitive and personal data that is getting shared mixed in with non-personal data. So I think
that's the second piece that judiciary needs to think about. I think the third piece and of course there's so
many things but these are my three is uh judiciary doesn't have the expertise to build these things in house
right. So they're going to rely on third party vendors. So to engender public trust, I think public procurement
has to happen in an open and transparent way. And there needs to be clear guidelines that are set up for
what is uh sort of the assessment of these systems on a regular basis by judiciary external auditors to

cases is that you know judges write by hand. So if we were all in a courtroom and you know I asked as a
lawyer I asked a witness where were you on the night of 21st April um the judge would stop us there write
this by hand and then the next thing would be dictated now as you can imagine this becomes a serious
bottleneck uh to court proceedings moving fast um and to that extent we've built AI transcription solutions
so that you don't have to write things by hand you can just um you know go about your witness deposition
and then you get a transcript of the same um by virtue of the process itself everything is verified on spot. uh
what we are seeing is that solutions like this which are simple that don't go into um you know sort of being
an AI judge or suggesting how to make a judgment are able to improve productivity at a daily and weekly
level by 2 to 3x which at scale could reduce case resolution timelines by 30 to 50% depending on case stage
and type right and so I think the big point uh that I'm trying to drive home is that even apart from the

that's available and not being suggestive, not giving uh any sort of judicial advice. Let's say if you're building
a WhatsApp chatbot for communities to find out about, you know, information about their cases, AI
systems cannot give advice today. That's not safe at all. That those things have not been audited yet. We

trust, I think what needs to happen is AI systems need to acknowledge when they don't know something.

around AI use by judges. What it's appropriate to use it for, what it's not appropriate to use it for, where
you are using it, what kind of safeguards, process checks need to be in place around its use. when courts are
acquiring AI technology whether it's for translation transcription administrative management research
purposes what kind of vetting auditing assessments of the system is being done at the point of um you
know um acquisition of this technology but also um you know post-deployment uh assessments and audits
that are being conducted um you know uh how open and transparent is public procurement. Um and then
what is the kind of transparency that is remaining? So first you have to put into place these safeguards.
Right? I think liability then becomes the next layer. I think uh at this point adoption of AI and courts is being
used as a tool for judges rather than direct impact where uh you know it's directly impacting citizen rights. I
mean there have been instances of compass and heart and victor other technologies which were deployed
in jurisdictions like the US, Brazil, the UK which didn't you know had their own sort of challenges. I think
people are more cognizant of it now. Um so I think li decision making I would say is largely still um vested in
judges. So I'm not sure where that liability comes in but I think overarchingly governments are increasingly
thinking about liability of AI systems more generally right not specific to AI use in judiciary but I think more
generally um and I think that is still an evolving conversation um many thought there's a lot of thinking

layer of infrastructure, fifth layer of data centers, energy. In the world of AI, which is the Fifth Industrial

industry priorities, quality, innovation and global positioning. [applause] So let me set this up uh by talking
about the brief context and then I'll um you know hand ask a few question of the panelists. Indian
pharmaceutical industry uh as we all know is globally recognized for its scale and reliability. India supplies
about 20% of the global generic medicines in about 60% of the vaccines reaching more than 190 countries.
This scale advantage has popularly uh made India the title of being called the pharmacy of the world. The
stated ambition for Vixid Bharat 2047 is to however build a USD 500 billion pharmaceutical industry several
fold more than it is today and that cannot happen by volume expansion alone. Over 2/3 of the global
pharmaceutical value pool uh sits in innovative medicines, new chemical entities as well as new biological
entities. A structural shift from volume to value is therefore imperative. This pivot is also supported by the
government and policy. The recent union budget emphasized biofarma shaky with increased allocations and
policy thrust for biologics, innovationled R&amp;D, advanced manufacturing and deep tech integration.
Artificial intelligence sits at the core of this transition not as a standalone solution but as an enabling layer
across discovery, development, manufacturing and patient engagement. This panel is therefore very timely

We are building various services related to various sectors like for example for education services,
healthcare, [music] agriculture, farmers, small businesses. So more than 2,000 services have been built up
as part of the [music] India's digital offering. So when we offer services to people, one very key focus of our
digital story has been on the issue of inclusion. Inclusion is several dimensions. One is of course ensuring
access to devices, ensuring access to connectivity, ensuring [music] access to to applications. But very key
aspect given the diversity of India is that services need to be available in all Indian languages because not
everybody speaks English or Hindi. So we have to ensure that people are able to access information in
services in their mother tongue. And the second aspect is that a lot of people would require services which
are voice enabled because we do have 900 million people in India who are online but then 500 million
people are not and for them to get onboarded very often they would struggle to go to a website or enter a
URL or download an app or navigate a capture. [music] They would need to ask like just like we talked to an
Alexa or a Siri that if a farmer wants to know what is the price of uh cotton [snorts] in the nearest money he
should be able to ask this question in his mother tongue get a reply back. [music] For example, even if we
say that there are 22 official languages, but each of the official languages have multiple variations. For
example, the Hindi that you speak in Lacuo and the Hindi that you speak in [music] uh in Hana and the Hindi
that you speak in Bihar not the same. There are local nuances associated with it. So [music] if you are if you
capture that those data sets then you will be able to build an AI application which is much more realistic. So
you have to kind of train models in the way people speak [music] and that would mean that having the data

sets in the right context. Cultural context also becomes very very important. All our customs, all our
traditions, all our food, our cuisine, the dresses, the diversity that we see in India is immense. So how do
you present that? So that the building [music] such rich data sets will help build Indian foundation models
which will then perform better in Indian situations compared to a chat GPT or a deep seek or any other uh
foundation model. So while chad GPT has made the common man notice AI but India has been working on
this for a long time. So currently in the India II mission we are working on several dimensions. What is very
strong about India and what is our key strength is about the talent that we have. The number of engineers
that we have, the number of startups that we have which are building AI based applications. So what the
India in India emission is trying to do is that number one set up India data sets platform which will provide
data sets across sectors in various languages which startups and entrepreneurs and researchers [music] can
use to build models and products. Number two, provide subsidized access to compute so that uh compute
does not remain as a challenge. And third, provide funding support for those who are building Indian
foundation models. [music] And we do hope that in the next 6 to9 months, we will have Indian foundation
models trained on Indian data sets. Maybe start to say working in few [music] sectors, healthcare or
agriculture or legal aid, but providing real service to people. [music] So by 2030 of course we hope to
become a AI force to [music] recon with. Currently we are much behind US and China but with the kind of
investments the government is making the kind of effort [music] the private sector is making and the kind of
energy with the startups have we do hope that we'll be able to move up and catch up with the leaders in
this field.

in hosting this hybrid meeting of the human capital working group under the India AI impact summit. India
is set to host the upcoming India AI impact summit in February 2026 at Bharat Mandapam, New Delhi. The
summit will focus on three sutras, people, planet and progress. This summit is fourth in line to a series of AI
summits in in the world. It follows the summits happened in UK, South Korea and France. The summit is
guided by seven chakras. Human capital, democratizing AI resources, economic growth and social good,
inclusion of social empowerment, safe and trusted AI, resilience, innovation and efficiency. The India AI
impact summit 2026 will host several flagship events and that includes AI pitchfest, research symposium, AI
expo, global innovation challenges for students, women and other participants and also showcasing
compendiums on the best use cases of AI in sectors like health, education and gender empowerment. This
gathering comes at a very particular important moment. Governments across the world are working to
ensure that the benefits of artificial intelligence are equitably shared, responsibly deployed, and firmly
anchored in public interest outcomes. The objective of the AI impact summit is to therefore ensure
democratization of AI resources and their equitable use across the masses especially in the countries of
global south. This Guhati convening on human capital will serve as a key regional and thematic precursor to
the India AI impact summit 2026 in New Delhi where national and global stakeholders will converge to
shape the future of inclusive, responsible and impactdriven AI. The outcomes from this working group will
directly inform the summit deliberations reinforcing India's commitment to building a workforce that is
resilient, future ready and empowered with AI for a country as large and diverse as India. We have to
ensure that AI is AI adoption is inclusive and affordable. Equally important is the need to build strong
human capital and a skilled workforce that can effectively develop, deploy and use these technologies. At
the India AI mission led by the Ministry of Electronics and IT, we are undertaking several initiatives to
develop compute capacity, our own indigenous data sets, models and target skilling and capacity building
efforts in AI. In this regard, the future skills pillar is the cornerstone of the India AI mission where we are
developing India's AI talent ecosystem and democratizing access to AI education and skilling nationwide.
The India AI mission looks forward to continued and strengthened collaboration with the government of
Assam, whether through supporting state-led AI use cases, facilitating access to national data sets and
models, or jointly advancing scaling and capacity building programs. Once again, I am very pleased and
thankful to be here on behalf of the Ministry of Electronics and IT and India AI mission and I look forward to
the outcome outcomes of this hybrid working group meeting on human capital. Thank you very much.

At its heart, friends, the human capital working group is guided by a simple idea, ensuring that the
transition to an AI enabled economy is inclusive and people centered. We recognize that human capital lies
at the core of its transition and that reskilling and upskilling are very essential for enabling workers and
institutions to adapt to technological change with dignity and opportunity. We are meeting at a very
defining moment in the human history. On one side, climate change, resource depletion, environmental
stress and geopolitical conflicts. On the other side, AI arguably the most powerful tool humanity has ever
created. The future is not about choosing between the growth and sustainability. It is about choosing
intelligence. This intelligence coming through this artificial intelligence makes us very potential to adopt.
Friends, artificial intelligence is rapidly transforming economies, labor markets and the institutions across
the world. The transformation brings extraordinary opportunities to rise productivity, unlock innovation and
exhilarate development. At the same time, it presents deep and complex challenges, particularly for
workers, enterprises, and communities that are least prepared for the speed and scale of change. Nearly
40% jobs globally are expected to be affected by AI in some form or the other. Yet, the capacity to respond
is far from equal. Uneven access to technology, skills, training systems and institutional readiness risks
widening existing divides between countries, between sectors and within societies. This is why the question
before us is not merely how fast AI is advancing but how we choose to shape its impact on the people. And
that is where this human capital working group among all the seven chakras becomes very important in the
AI impact summit which is going to happen in February 2026 at Bharatam andapam in New Delhi within the
broader framing of the impact summit anchored in the triad of people planet and progress. You can see that
again people planet and progress. Our focus today is firmly on people. The human capital challenge is about
ensuring that technological pro progress translates into dignity, opportunity and resilience. The true
measure of success will not be the sophistication of our systems alone but whether people are able to
adopt, participate and thrive in an AI enabled economy. My call to all the youngsters is you need to
embrace this technology otherwise you will be left behind. AI must therefore be understood not only as a
tool for efficiency but as a force that reshapes how we think about work learning and inclusion. Friends,
learning itself has changed and thanks to COVID during that time we actually leap frogged the use of
technology in educational institutions. At the same time, learning has also been reshaped because of thanks
to national education policy 2020 where learning is happening in many folds now. Not like the time when
we were students. When we were students, it was all teacher assisted learning. Today learning happens
through self-arning, collaborative learning, experiential learning and all these different modes have enabled
the young people to learn quickly and in a better perspective. So across the meetings of the human capital
working group, we had several of them actually a clear message has emerged. While there is a strong
agreement that reskilling and upskilling are essential, there is also recognition that it is traditional
approaches, fragmented training programs, narrow skill pipelines and short-term interventions are no
longer sufficient and also very importantly the infrastructure required for training needs to be strengthened
in India in particular because the two countries which are very very advanced in these areas are America
and China. So based on the discussions we have come up with two documents. One is the equitable AI
transition playbook. The second is voluntary guiding principles in the age of artificial intelligence. So these
two documents will be ready and it will be presented during the February meeting in New Delhi where
honorable prime minister is going to chair that uh the conference. The pace of AIdriven change calls for a
shift towards lifelong learning ecosystems at and are accessible across regions and socioeconomic groups

and responsive to evolving labor market needs. In this process, national education policy 2020 also
emphasizes on lifelong learning. Friends, it also requires us to value adaptability, judgment, and humancentered capabilities alongside technical skills. Most importantly, this transition must be just and inclusive
without deliberate effort. Those already facing structural disadvantages risk being left further behind
particularly women, informal sector workers and communities in developing economy. To bridge this gap
under this woman working group, we have conducted national workshops across the country. More than 20
workshops have been held at different institutions, engineering colleges and emphasizing more on skilling,
upskilling using artificial intelligence. Even the curriculum modernization is done at all engineering colleges
and I would like to tell you that our institutions particularly in engineering technology have embraced this

different constraints, none can manage AIdriven workforce transformation alone. So it is a time to work
together. Shared learning and collaboration are therefore essential to an equitable global response. Our
discussions are not about prescribing uniform solutions but about learning from diverse experiences and
identifying approaches that can be adopted across contexts. India's approach to the impact summit reflects
this inclusive ethos. Friends, by placing people at the center of AI vision, India seeks to demonstrate that

technology department in the government of Asam. I speak to you more today as a student of the profound

shifts that we are witnessing. We are gathered in Gojhati today, a region that that represents the
aspirations of the global south as we try to deliberate on this very complex and very timely and important
topic of human capital. With this imminent age of AI, we we all been speaking about how it is like a new
industrial revolution altogether. But the integration of AI into this economy would represent a very different
kind of a discontinuity. This is the sheer cognitive velocity that you're going to see which is happening here.
Earlier when we were automating, we were automating the manpower itself. But here the talk now is about
mo about um automating the the cognitive task itself. The change is so profound that we had uh tech
leaders like Sundar Pichai saying that this discovery of AI, this use of AI is possibly going to have a far greater
impact than possibly fire and discovery of electricity itself. But that is amazing more than fire and electricity.
But this change that is happening is also happening at a very rapid pace and the way it is spreading it is very
likely that there would lead to a far larger differences in the capability across organizations and across
sectors unless a conscious decision is taken and we act deliberately to eress this increasing inequality that
will happen. So the question before the working group is not just whether AI will change human capital of
that I have absolutely no doubt AI is going to have an impact. The bigger question now in the case of human
capital is what does human capital even mean when machines can perform large parts of the cognitive
work. Now that is the interesting part. What does human capital mean? Historically over the last decades
the situation was very simple for most of us. For most organizations we require that human capital is a form
of capital. So you have people you educate them. You make sure I people have seen in the data that there is
a great premium for having a college education. So you put in a lot of effort to make sure that there's no
dropout at the schooling level and people go on to college. That was a very simple model. There is
technology which is advancing fast and if you skill up your people that will lead to progress, greater
productivity etc. But with this kind of change that we are seeing now the way AI is coming in and frankly not
just because of AI if you look at technological progress in general and if you go by the powerful arguments
that uh the Darren Asimaglu and Simon Johnson's not so recent book on power and progress they
document large number of cases where over times historically technological progress has not always
correlated with a prosperity which is shared with everyone. They argue and very rightly so I'm convinced by
the arguments there that technology can displace labor and concentrate wealth unless societies
deliberately shape the direction of innovation and they shape it in a manner to augment human worker
rather than replace it. So the focus should be that on augmentation than replacement. So this is going to be
the biggest challenge and the biggest decision that all of us need to be able to take and if we accept that
augmentation must be the real goal that our job is with AI that is coming in we're not looking at
replacement we're looking at augmenting the human capability if we do so then the current formulation or
the current thought of looking at human capital that would appear to be a bit too narrow and we may have
to expand it further and to think in terms of human capability and human agency. So I guess the premium
will shift towards not what you know but have you learn to learn and how fast can you learn and acquire
new skills and exercise judgment. There is no way that the human learning can match the the rate of
learning and the speed of learning that AI is showing. Isn't it? So what we are going to look forward is
something like learning with the AI and using AI as a cognitive multiplier. So the question will no longer be
what skills you possess but how effectively can you use AI to solve new problems while remaining in control
of the intent and the outcome. The hardest problem that the working group or anyone is going to face
going forward is how do you skill humans fast enough when AI itself is killing itself at an exponential pace
for me the last one year has been magical. I I still love to code and I remember when I first picked up GitHub
copilot and said wow it was early Jan Feb it is doing a very smart autocomplete. Well, autocomplete was
simple. Then very soon you start seeing that it can take your intent and write the next line of code. I'm
talking about just Jan Feb last year and now we are at a stage where frankly coding in Python directly or any
programming tool itself appears to be so ancient times. Now you got to do so-called wipe coding. Over the
last couple of days, I was able to build applications. Instead of coding, you're just talking with an with the
prompt and you're able to build applications. So this is the rate at which we are seeing the capability of AI
increasing. So are we really going to be capable of skilling humans at a very fast rate when AI itself skills at a
far far higher rate? This is going to be a very big challenge. So in such a situation will the conventional model
of scaling with a a four-year degree and even a four-year degree from a premium institution like IIIT would
that hold any value over a very long period of time? Highly unlikely, isn't it? What we are seeing is that the
half-life of knowledge has been dropping rapidly. Maybe a generation ago you acquired a set of knowledge,
you graduated and maybe it was good for a lifetime. Then you say the half life is half life is in a matter of

decades and now they're saying in a couple of years halfife of the knowledge and what you've learned half
of it has become irrelevant and in core technology sectors it could even be in a period of months so when
skills and knowledge that you have are going to expire so fast are going to lose their relevance so fast what
kind of a solution are we looking for and that's why I was very happy when I was looking at how a working
group is looking at it and they're talking about microscaling a digital public infrastructure of sort you know
where we are able to keep these catalog of skills and keep track of these micro credentials that people
acquire. So there no longer can you acquire a set of skill and bask on it or course on it for a very long period
of time. It is microsklling and continuous learning is something that we'll have to do all across. Now this
whole idea and importance of skilling is even more important for what we call as a global south for us. I've
seen statistics which say you know that develop developed countries maybe 30% 40% of the jobs are going
to be impacted but you know like us in India maybe only 10% of the jobs get impacted that is not something
to be very happy about frankly when I think about it it's all because most of us most of our country is
engaged in a lower cognitive capacity or lower value added activities which did not take that kind of an
cognitive input while in the test high value activities which are more cognitive but more easily attacked or
not attacked wrong word more easily used or supplanted or complemented by AI. So if we say that only
10% of our jobs are going to be impacted adversely what we are missing is that the path that normally
would be followed is that from the manual labor we go on to a you know a manufacturing kind of a job and
go on to a services sector using greater cognitive capacity. this path of development that was available
would no longer be available and effectively we end up into a you know a an early pre-industrialization of
sort you know like the industrialization itself has come to an end because there is no reason for us to go
that path because all of manufacturing and high-tech activity and cognitive activity can be done centrally in
a very small place. So we need not and should not be complicent of the fact that smaller number of jobs in
the global south are going to be impacted rather we should be even more concerned about it. So what
should we be do doing about that? Without doubt this idea of um skilling human capability, AI literacy these
become extremely important. AI literacy is to be considered as absolutely mandatory. It is going to be a
universal competence that all of us is to have and not just engineers, citizens, workers, public servants,
everyone. We all need to understand AI. How does it work? how bio enters and how do we apply it in real

can code so all of us have an AI co-pilot and that becomes an integral part of the learning and scaling of

dependency where our own cognitive capability it is atrophy. So how are you going to build skills which call
for using AI but at the same time not becoming so dependent that your own ability to monitor it to control
the future task and what is the intent the final intent should be yours the final outcome should be yours and

with their business with their work with their education with very nearly every human activity that involves

amounts of data but it's static since it was static it can only answer questions based on what it has been fed
And if we are going to use such a system dynamically for day-to-day usage, we need to feed it dynamic data.
Data that is current, not what is been fed in during its so-called learning phase. Now, since these systems
are huge, we're talking about billions of parameters or edges in a graph, if you will. It's a it requires lot of
computing power. So it is not feasible to keep adding new data on the fly so to speak to the system uh in
the way that we fed the initial data that is through learning. We can't make it keep learning with new inputs
because learning process is very compute intensive. There are remember billions of parameters that who
each of which has a weight each of which weight has to be changed for every potentially every weight has
to be changed for every new input data input. So there have been talk about fine-tuning such a system. So

can have more information. So if we feed more information through the prompt that could be the dynamic
data and therefore we could use the system uh with current data but then you know the so the so-called
prompt engineering is an area where now people have been working on and try to see how to write good
prompts how long can the prompt be. So they're trying to make the input even longer and longer to these
systems so that you can have huge prompts. uh then the other directions has said okay if we want a domain
specific LLM we can instead of through the prompt we can create what are called vector databases of the
domain specific data and then when given a prompt we convert that prompt to a vector find similar vectors
in the database we created and feed these similar vectors to the LLM as inut put like as a prompt. So
instead of giving in a huge amount of data which may not be feasible, we find the relevant data through the
vector database and we feed it to the through a prompt and we get some we get data we get answers
specific to the data that we have in the vector databases. So this is called RA. And so this is one of the
technique that has been used to try to make it dynamic. But then we are now at a stage where we it has
been realized that this is not sufficient. We need systems beyond just an LLM. LLM can understand English

a prompt comes in anyway the prompt does not go to an LLM to begin with. It goes to some other software

the panel. Good afternoon everyone. Thank you for joining us for this timely discussion on gender
responsive strategies for the AI transition. Much of the global conversation around AI and the future of
work has focused on productivity gains, innovation and competitiveness. What has received far less
attention is who bears the cost of this transition and whether AI risks deepened the existing inequities in
the labor markets. Unlike earlier waves of automation that primarily disrupted manufacturing and manual
labor, this AI wave is transforming cognitive, clerical and administrative work as well. roles such as customer
support, human resources, data processing, and back office functions. These roles have historically served
as a critical entry point for women into formal employment, especially in the services sector as well as in all
emerging economies. What makes this moment particularly challenging is that AIdriven disruption is often
invisible and it disproportionately affects women especially those concerned in the midscale white collar

roles as well. Today's discussion is about designing AI transition frameworks intentionally so that it protects
livelihoods, ensures mobility and opens new opportunities rather than closing doors. We will explore what
gender responsive AI governance actually means in practice, how skilling and workforce transitions need to
be rethought and what long-term policy frameworks and safeguards are required to ensure that the
workforce transition is inclusive and equitable. I would encourage our panelists to share their perspectives
and wisdom drawing on evidence, lived experiences and examples of what has worked and what has not. I
also urge the audience to ask questions in the session that we'll have after the question and questions with

very much. Uh it it's a privilege to be here this afternoon part of this panel. Let me answer this question in
two parts. As an academician, let me begin with a very simple but unsettling question. In the cognitive age,
in the cognitive age, the very signals of learning that we have been traditionally using may not be
appropriate at all anymore. Let me explain. Most of the time we measure or understand learning through

cognitive tasks like asking you to write an essay, solve a numerical or write a bit of code. But in the cognitive
edge, a system, an AI system can do it immediately. Now is this a question of plagarism that you bring a
solution from there? A question of academic integrity? I do not think so. We have a more unsettling sort of
situation here. A structural change is required. We can no longer be measuring learning by the final product
of what is coming out of a cognitive task that's given to you. We have to be very careful in evaluating the
process through which this cognitive task has been completed by the student in the age of an AI system
helping you. That's one thing. The other thing that I need to emphasize in is it is no longer a question of
whether AI is part of your education system or not. The question is what and which type of AI do you want
to integrate into the learning in colleges and schools. So I have a very simple phrase here. It cannot be any
longer by AI. Solutions by AI is something we are not looking at. We are looking at solutions with AI where
cognition where decision making authority is not delegated any longer to the AI system. It's still with us. The
reasoning with this as professor Gautam Burwa sir had very well highlighted today morning it's about core
fundamental knowledge domain knowledge still be part of our training yet it would be a new era of training
where you need to know how to use AI to augment your capabilities. So as Gopinat uh in the morning had
highlighted it's about augmentation of our capabilities to really increase our efficiency. It's not augmenting
our capabilities to learn and replace learning. It is about augmenting our capabilities to improve our
performance. So the essence of augumentation in an cognitive age is very interesting and very important to
realize. It's an augumentation of human in the loop with human taking decision making, cognition,
reasoning. Whereas machine substituting for speed, for pattern recognition, for accuracy and that marriage
is going to work wonders. If we train students that way, it would be something that would come up in in
years to come. Um, we have to be very careful that we do not miss the bus by only training people to work
by AI. That's something that would push us decades back. We have to train our workforce, our human
capital to work with AI where we can realize the limitations of the models, the biases, the cognitive power.
So it's it it has to be that way rather than oneway traffic of just using it as a tool. So that's one point. The
next point that you wanted is where is regular things relegated in education? So in education lot of people

used and AI tools is used for grading for keeping record of things so that those are mundane task that are
done but that's a different story my fundamental idea that remains pertinent to our discussion today is that

more activities are sent. If the teacher requires less activities and less activities are sent and this ability to

classroom then the AI can just be a back background support. In the case where the teacher is struggling,
the AI can provide much more support and that ability ability to to have flexibility is really really important.

transparency can lead to an insight but from the insight to the actual decision that judgment has to stay

group AI summit. We now begin the technical session one titled the architecture of the national language
transition mission. This session will discuss the technological foundations of India's national efforts to build
inclusive language egonistic digital public infrastructure. It is my privilege to invite our distinguished speaker
for this session. Professor Mitesh Capra, professor at IIT Madras and head of AI4 Bharat, a leading initiative
driving research and deployment of Indian language AI systems at scale. Professor Kapra is joining us online.

got started around four years back and the goal was to build the foundation to enable machines to
understand the rich and diverse lives of Indians. Right? which of course means a large part of this diversity
comes from languages, right? So there was a lot of emphasis on rapidly accelerating uh the development of
language technology or AI uh technology for Indian languages, right? And we at AI for Bharat were an
important part of this journey where we were the data management unit which means a large amount of
the data that was collected and released as public good through Bashini was uh collected by our teams right
and now when this mission started right and like I'll just interchange between the journey of AI for Bharat
and Bashini uh one simple question was that why is it that Indian language technology today is not par with

uh English right and the simple no-brainer answer to that was that there are no uh not enough data sets for
Indian languages. Okay, that's that's one part of the problem. Uh the other is when models are built uh the
deep tech industry sort of uh sways between these two extremities. One extremity is profitering where
English and uh certain politically important languages are given more importance politically commercially
important languages and the this is the profitering side and the other is the positioning side or posturing
side uh where there is emphasis on hey we should be inclusive for thousand languages 10,000 languages
and so on right and in both these extremities Indian languages uh and if we talk about the u languages
included in the eth schedule Bianium constitution which are 22 languages lie somewhere in between right
because when you're looking at only the commercially rich languages then these languages will not get
attention and similarly when you're talking about thousand languages 10,000 languages you cannot zoom
into a specific set of languages that you care about right so that's where uh we come in we said that we'll
focus on the 22 languages which cover around 90 to 95% of the population of India and make our modeling
choices also accordingly right another reason for Indian language technology to be behind behind is there is
no incentive uh for people to build uh models for Indian languages because there are no good benchmarks.

benchmarks for say Marathi, Gujarati, Telu, Hindi and on these benchmarks you are not your models are
not doing well so you better improve right so that's why evaluation is also an important part of the journey

data sets, build multilingual models uh which cater to Indian languages and then also design evaluation

studies have shown which is used for machine translation is not uh very u appropriate for Indian languages

uh generation of LLMs and hence we had thought of these as silos that there should be speech recognition

questions, right? It's a very limited domain and the farmer is wants to know the price of certain uh crops in

&gt;&gt; Have you ever wondered how traditional textbooks can hinder the learning experience of children
with ADHD? Meet Vun, a bright and curious student who faces this very challenge. As Vun opens his
textbooks to learn, he finds himself lost in a sea of distractions. The small font, cluttered illustrations, and
large amount of text overwhelmed his already limited attention span. But why does this happen? Vin has
recently been diagnosed with ADHD. ADHD stands for attention deficit hyperactivity disorder. Through
meticulous experiments and a longitudinal study, we discovered design parameters that could transform
Vun's learning journey. The perfect blend of font size, type, line spacing, and background color became the
key to unlocking his focus. These parameters were then compiled into a set of our visual design guidelines
to redesign textbooks. So now I will have the privilege to basically share with you what kind of national

felicitate the Go. We now proceed to the technical session two titled the reverse engineering approach to AI
education organized by the NEST cluster. The northeastern science and technology cluster is an initiative of
the ministry of development of northeastern region donor through NEC which will be implemented by IIT
Goati. The cluster aims to promote research innovation and skill development in theatic areas of
semiconductor and artificial intelligence, grassroot technologies, bamboo based technology and
biodegradable plastics. The cluster aims to connect academia, industry, research and government agencies
to empower the northeastern region, youth and women through skill development and entrepreneurship.
The cluster was launched in the month of November 2025 in the August presence of honorable union
minister Shri Jotiraa Cindy G. Now we will have a short documentary of the nest cluster. Welcome to the
dawn of innovation and collaboration in Northeast India. Presenting the Northeastern Science and
Technology Nest Cluster, a section 8 company. An initiative conceived as a transformative platform to
promote research, innovation and skill development by the Ministry of Development of Northeastern
Region through Northeastern Council at IIT Goadi. Anchored in the philosophy of inclusive and
technologydriven growth, the cluster seeks to serve as a vital link between academia, research institutions,
and industry with the ultimate goal of fostering regional development. With four dynamic verticals,
nurturing grassroots innovations, not limiting to advancing artificial intelligence and semiconductor
technologies, championing bamboo based solutions, and promoting biodegradable plastics and plastic
waste management and much more. The cluster is a beacon of hope where innovation meets opportunity.
The cluster aims to work through a self-sustainable model with the philosophy of skill development of youth
of northeastern region in relevant fields of science and technology. We empower aspiring entrepreneurs
with specialized training, certification programs, patenting, technology licensing and customized

development in our tinkering labs, all aimed at fostering startups and accelerating innovation in the region.
Join us as we unlock the true potential of Northeast India through technology, entrepreneurship and
collaboration. IIT Gojhati feels honored that the honorable Union Minister of Development of Northeastern
region Shri Jodhi M. Cindia G is with us for the grand inauguration of the Northeastern Science and
Technology Cluster. This session will explore innovative approaches to preparing future AI workforces by
encouraging problem solving, curiosity and hands-on engagement with real world challenges from early
stages of education. It is my privilege to invite professor Amit Akar, professor department of computer
science and engineering IIT Gojhati to deliver the session. I invite professor Akar to kindly take the stage and
begin the session. Okay. Fine. Hello. Good morning everyone. Uh welcome to this session on reverse
engineering approach to AI [clears throat] education. I know that I'm standing between you and your lunch.
So I will be quick, I'll be brief. Uh and I'll keep this session as much interactive as possible. So just a show of
hands. Can I know how many of you are school students here? Are there any school students? A few in the
back. Few in the corner in the front. And oh, there are also many at the top. Okay. And how many of you are
college students here? Okay. Quite a many of you. Okay. Fine. So uh what I have done is that I have geared
this talk mainly for school and college students. Uh so we'll start really from basics and then move further.
Okay. In between if you have any questions uh any doubts uh feel free to ask the questions. Uh do we have
any volunteers here around with the mic? Yeah. So there are volunteers around with the mic. If you have
anything to ask in between feel free to interrupt me. Okay. So let's start. Okay. So just to give brief
introduction about me. I work here at IIT Goati. It's been about 15 years and it's a happy ride and we do
many interesting things in AI. Okay. So let's start to understand the title. Okay. The title is about uh reverse
engineering approach to AI education. Right. So let's first understand what do we actually mean by
engineering. Okay. So if you look at the ethmology of word engineering, it comes from the Latin word
engineer which means to devise to to make something. Okay. Uh so typically what do you expect an
engineer to do is is to build systems to build something that works to solve a problem. Okay. And if you look
around there are many problems. Okay. So here is example of one problem uh a couple of problems that I
have shown uh on your left you will see a problem that many of the remote villages they do not have good
connectivity and the school students uh they perhaps have to swim the river or a stream to go to the
school. Okay. Uh and there are many such social problems around. Okay, we as an engineers uh we have to
deise the solutions for these problems. So somebody might go and build a bridge, somebody might come up
with some other innovative solution. Uh so what engineers are supposed to do is they are supposed to look
around for the problems and solve them. Okay, on the right hand side uh this is the problem perhaps none
of us have faced in the recent years uh about the food shortages. Okay, so a few decades ago India had a lot
of food shortage. uh but again we did tremendous work in uh improving our full yield through the science
and engineering and we could solve the problem. Okay so engineering is about solving the problem. Some
of you are school students and who might take up the career. Uh so I highly encourage you to think about
engineering as the potential career. [clears throat] Second thing is if engineering is about a process of
moving from problem to a solution reverse engineering is exactly the opposite process. Okay, where you
have a working system uh and you are trying to understand why it works, how it works and you're trying to
understand the logic behind it. Okay, uh and there are two interesting examples here. On your left, uh there
is a photo from a temple called Somnata Temple in Karnataka which I just visited a couple of days ago. Uh
the temple is constructed in 13th century. Okay. And if you look at the pillars of the temple, uh it consists of
exact concentric circles. Okay. So back in 13th century we did not have any machinery uh that could actually
create the pillars of this exact circular shape. Okay. So we see this is a as a system which was built. Okay. We
see the temple is around but we have no clue how this might have been made. Okay. So any any guess how
this might have been made? So these are not approximate circles. These are exact circles. Okay. You can go
and do the measurements. Okay. Okay. And these are the circles of various red uh sizes. Any guesses how
things would have been made? Because this is what we have to do for any other system that we see. It's a
system that is there and we question how it might have been made. Okay. Are you aware of something
called a soap stone? Soap stone is a special kind of stone which is found in the southern Karnataka. When
you extract it out, it is as soft as sue. Okay? So, you can literally carve it the way you want it. But as it gets
exposed to the air and temperature, slowly it hardens and over a few months uh it becomes as hard as any
other rock. Okay. So when they excavated this uh soap rock uh rock soap uh they could easily make it into
concentric circles using the pottery kind of techniques and once it was prepared then it was installed back.
Okay. So that's the reverse engineering. You see the system and then you understand how it was made.

Okay. Second uh on your right is example of another system that every one of uses all the time the human
brain uh but we know very little about how exactly the brain functions. Okay. In the past century or so, we
have made quite some advances in understanding uh how the brain works. But a lot of secrets are still
hidden. Okay. So when we talk about reverse engineering anything, what we are trying to do is uh
understand how exactly the system was made. Okay. Third word in the title is is about intelligence. Okay.
Uh so this whole summit is is about AI which is artificial intelligence. uh within that what exactly is the
intelligence? Okay, so [clears throat] intelligence is not about doing a specific job, a specific work. Okay, the
idea of intelligence is to be able to do anything. Okay, you should be able to acquire any new skills. If you go
and look in the history of computing in 70s and 80s, we had many systems called expert systems that could
do one specific job well. So you had expert systems that would work as a dentist, expert system that might
work as a tax consultant. They would do one specific thing in a very narrowly defined, brittle way, in a
hardcoded way. Okay, compared to that the systems that we are building today, they are quite different.
Okay, they are not limited to one particular task. They are not rigid. They are not predefined. But they can
do many different things. [clears throat] And uh here are three examples that actually tell us uh how
intelligence has evolved in the past three decades. Okay. So on your left top uh you might see a photo uh
people who are of my age perhaps they will recognize uh what's that photo is about. That's Gary Kasparov
who was the world champion and in mid '90s there was a famous uh match between Gary Kasparov and the
Deep Blue system from IBM 1997. Okay. And in 1997 the deep blue system from IB IBM could defeat Gary
Kasparo. Okay. And that was a big news that finally computers are able to defeat human uh expert in the
game of chess. Okay. But the thing with the IBM deep blue was that it was a system that was based on
simply doing a brute force kind of search. Okay. So it could play chess but it could do only that thing and
nothing else. Okay. It was doing simply a very fast computation as compared to the human and it could
defeat the chess master. Okay. Fast forward to 2015. Uh do you recognize the second image on the right
hand side? It was another match between a human expert and a computing system. Okay. This time instead
of chess it was the game of go. Okay. And the person that you see in the picture is Lee Sudall who was the
world champion in the game of Go. And he had to play against a system called Alph Go which was
developed by Deep Mind. Okay. Alph Go was far more superior to the [clears throat] IBM's deeply uh but
still it could play only one particular game which was the game of Go. But instead of doing the brute force
search and brute force calculation, it was actually a system that was trained to learn from the millions of Go
games that were played before. Okay. So now we move from hardcoded search to a system that could learn
from millions of games before in the matter of two decades. Okay. So from 95 to approximately 2015. Okay.
And the third image that you see which is below which is Alpha Zero AI. It's another uh system again from
deep mind but it's even a step further. Okay. Uh so it can play not just the game of go but many other
games such as chess uh and other difficult games and it doesn't need a large number of games to train it.
Okay. So for example Alph Go it required millions of games that are played before to understand the
strategies for playing the game of go effectively. That's not the case with the Alpha Zero. Okay? All it needs
is the description of the rules of the game. It will start with absolutely no experience, absolutely no training,
and it will go on playing millions and millions of games with itself. From that, it will understand what are the
good strategies, what are the bad strategies, and eventually within a matter of days, it would go and beat
any human expert. So from 1997 to 2026 look at the evolution of the intelligence from a hardcoded system
that can do brute force search to a system that just needs a description of what needs to be done. It can do
any game anything on its own. Okay. So that's what the intelligence is about. It's about acquiring new skills
doing anything new. Okay. The next thing is is artificial because AI of course all of us are intelligent. uh that's
what we would like to believe but we are naturally intelligent because we are product of nature but when
we talk about anything artificial we are typically talking about anything that is man-made that does not exist
in nature readily and again here are two examples that you can see one is the artificial limb so many people
they lose their limbs due to accident or some medical condition and typically we replace them with the
artificial limb okay when we say that any artificial system is a replacement is an alternative ative for a
natural system. What is it that we expect that artificial system to do? Should it mimic the input output
relation or should it mimic the way of functioning? Okay, that depends on what is it that we want. Okay. Uh
on your right there is second example uh which was recently developed by a company in Serbia. uh this
particular algae which is developed in a box is considered as a replacement for trees. We talk about trees as
a solution for climate change to reduce the carbon dioxide level in our air. Uh but same thing can be
achieved by this particular box instead of planting the trees. Okay. So it's a system uh that will mimic the

trees at the input output level. It will take in the carbon dioxide, release the oxygen outside, but it's not a
tree. It will not do many other things that trees do. It will do the exact same thing that we want. So when
we talk about anything artificial, uh we should be clear about what is it that we expect it to do. Do we
expect it to follow the logic the working of the natural system or do we expect it to follow just the input
output mapping of the natural systems? Okay. And the last part in the title is about the education. Okay. Uh
this is an academic institute and education is what we deal with every day. Uh so education is about getting
certain skills training people. But which skills are important depends on the requirements of the society.
Okay. So perhaps 200 years ago skills that were important they might not be that important. 200 years ago
our society was primarily based on agriculture and we would expect a large part of our society to be well
trained in agriculture. Now in 2026 uh a large part of our society deals with science, deals with finance,
deals with technology and we expect a large part of our society uh to have skills in those domains. So
required skills they change with time. Okay. So putting all those things together. Okay. So what we want to
do okay we want to reverse engineer AI okay so we want to look at the AI systems that work once we look
at them we want to reverse engineer them we want to look at them and understand why this we need to
build the educational programs that help people to build those AI skills okay so that's that's the agenda for
reverse engineering the AI systems for education purpose okay so any questions any doubts till this or
everybody is sleeping quietly. Okay, let's move. Okay, so uh if you look around, there are many many
working AI systems all around. Uh I think every one of us is carrying a mobile phone in their pocket. Uh and
I'm sure it has at least a few AI apps already installed. You might have chat GPT, you might have Geminy,
you might have Microsoft Copilot. Uh you might have multiple image generation uh systems. Uh so we
don't have to look further for AI systems that are successful that are working. Uh but beyond personal use,
AI systems are deployed even for things are far more critical. Okay, such as healthcare. Okay. So if you are
following the news uh just last week as has announced uh a clinical decision support system that will be
installed across thousands of governments. You might be generating images using these AI tools or you
might be generating videos using these AI tools and your typical description is a text where you describe
how you want the image or how you want the video and output is image or video. So what we have to
understand is how exactly this bridging happens uh converting your text to numbers then all the processing
in terms of the numbers inside the AI system and once it comes up with its output in terms of number
converting that number again to the image or video. Okay. So uh we have to understand how this uh
translation happens. Okay. So again if you go little in the history before 2010 uh we were not that great in
representing text images in terms of the numerical format uh we did not have methods that would capture
the essence capture the meaning of this data and convert them to numbers. For example, if I take word
good and word nice, if I compare their spellings, uh the spellings are quite different. But if I look at their
meaning, the meaning of word good and meaning of word nice is quite similar. So when I convert these
number uh words to numbers, what should happen is that the representation should be quite similar. Okay.
Similarly, if I take image of a car which is of say green color and an image of another car which is of say black
color. If I just compare their images, the images would be quite different in terms of the color. The cars
might have different shape. Okay, but finally both of them are cars. So when I convert both of these images
to number to a representation, they should be similar. Okay, and this was the crucial part that we could
achieve in last 15 years. We could convert any type of data to the numerical representation while
understanding its essence while understanding its semantics while understanding its meaning. Okay. Once
that we could achieve, we could make rapid progress in the AI. Okay. Uh because this is a session with quite
limited time and diverse audience. Uh I would avoid going into the details of how do we convert any type of
data to the numeric representation. But I I see there are many engineering students around here. Uh you
should definitely go through such methods and understand them in detail uh to understand how exactly
rate of any modality goes as input to the AI systems and from those AI systems how do we get the rate of
any modality. Fine. So inspiration for the AI is of course the human brain. So if you look at the human brains
these are some of the important facts about the human brain. Our human brain consists of something
called neurons. And a typical human brain has about 10^ 11 neurons which is roughly around 100 billion
neurons. Okay. So you can think about neurons as uh uh a sort of processing units that take signal as the
input and these neurons are connected to each other and each neuron has about 10 power four
connections which means that each neuron is connected to about 10,000 other neurons. So now try to
imagine our brain which looks quite small physically. Inside that we have about 100 billion processing units
and each processing unit is connected to about 10,000 other units. So it's a large network that we have in

our brain. Okay. And each of these unit can switch on and switch off. You can roughly think about it as
output being one or output being zero. So whenever output changes from 1 to 0 or 0 to 1, we say that the
neuron has switched. Okay. How much time does it take for a neuron to switch its state from 0 to 1 or 1 to
0? It's about 10^ 3 seconds, which is about a millisecond. Okay. So any neuron can change its state in in
about 1 millisecond. So [clears throat] you take a second and divide it into thousand parts. That's that's the
millisecond that we are talking about. So that slow change happens. And whenever we typically see
anything and we have to take a decision for example if I see face of a person and I have to take a guess
about what might be the age of this person, what might be the gender of this person or what might be the
emotions of this person. If I have to make such judgments, what is the typical time required by humans to
take such decisions? It is about 10^ minus 1 seconds which is 1/10th of a second. Okay? So each neuron
takes about 10^ minus3 time to switch on or off and the final result comes out in about 10^ -1 seconds. It
means that typically we get 100 steps 10^ two steps to do any task. It's a very small number of steps that
our brain takes. Okay, only about 100 steps which means that there must be a lot of parallel processing that
is happening in our brain. It's not only one sequential program, one sequential code that runs through our
brain. The processing happens in parallel in multiple places but a total 100 steps in terms of time that we
get and we we get the answer. And then there is distributed representation of various things which means
that if we compare our brain with the hard disks that we use in our computers on our hard disk we can
locate specific part specific area where certain thing is stored. Okay. Okay, so you have files, images, videos
in various folders. Uh you can always open your hard disk and point out one specific physical part of the
hard disk where your data is stored. Sometimes you get bad sectors in your hard disk and certain part of
data is lost. Right? Uh so our hard disk doesn't have distributed representation. It has representation which
is a specific. Okay? Compared to that human brain has a distributed representation which means that a
certain concept a certain thing is represented multiple times in different locations in a distributed manner
and whenever we do things there is no explicit coding algorithm. Okay for example if you go and look at a
simple programming problems that we deal with for example if I want to sort the numbers there are
specific algorithms for that right? uh you can go and pick up the smallest number first then the second
smallest third smallest and so on. Okay, that is one way to sort the numbers and you can go and list maybe
10 other such algorithms that will sort the numbers in the ascending order or descending order. Which
means that whenever we write computer programs, there is a predefined way of solving the problem.
Compared to that, if we look at our brain, there is no such predefined algorithm. The way you do a certain
work uh and I do the same work, the logic might be quite different. Even forget about across the person
when we do the same work at different times we might use different algorithm. So most of the times we
cannot tell how we do certain thing. If you look at a person and you guess the age of the person, can you
tell me exact step-by-step algorithm? We cannot tell. We might tell the clues that we pick up. Is the
person's face wrinkled or not? Uh and so on. So we can go on picking up the clues but we cannot tell the
exact step-by-step algorithm. So those are the pecularities of the human brain. Okay. And all this happens
with extremely low power consumption. The amount of power consumed by human brain is about 20 watt
which is almost same as the typical light bulb that we put in our house. Okay. So the single light bulb that
you would put in your room the amount of power that it consumes approximately same amount of power is
consumed by our human brain and that's the brain that has done all the creative things that we see around
including development of AI. Okay. So this brain is the inspiration for developing the artificial intelligence.
This is the natural intelligence that we have. Okay. So compared to that let's see where our current AI sits.
Okay. So current AR has about 10^ 9 neurons which is about 1 billion neurons. So I'm talking about the
models which are the frontier models the most recent models. Okay. So we have about 10^ 9 neurons. So
compared to human brains we are about two orders of magnitudes away. So human brain has 10^ 11. AI
currently has about 10^ 9. Okay. And we have about 10^ 3 connections. Compared to that the human brain
has about 10^ four connections which means that we have about one order of magnitude less onetenth of

Okay. So our artificial neurons are quite faster. How much faster? They are million times faster than the

answer in in milliseconds which is 10^ minus 3 which is about 100 times faster. Okay. Uh and just like
