no explicit coding algorithm to solve any task. So when you give input to a neural network, it will do the

the difference between the current AI models versus the the human brain. Okay. So let's try to open up this
black box and understand how these models work. So these models uh the current models they use
artificial neural networks which means that uh we are going to have neurons which model the neurons in
our brain. We are going to have connections which model the connections in our brain and then we are
going to have processing that again is a model of the way brain functions. Okay. And whenever we talk
about modeling uh we inevitably introduce the error. Okay. So there is a very famous quote by a statistician
professor box. It says that all models are wrong. Some models are useful. Okay. So whenever you create
model of anything uh you are going to be wrong. You cannot have a model that is able to 100% replicate the
original system. Uh you will introduce errors but the model turns out to be useful. Okay. If you want
example uh one of the example is is you might have solved a problem of projectile motion in your 11th and
12th. The engineering students will remember projectile motion. You throw the ball at a certain angle and
then you ask at what distance it will fall down. after how much time it will fall fall down to what height it
will go. Okay. Uh and while solving such a problem you create a very simple model of the the earth and
everything you say that earth is the only entity that is exerting the force of gravity. You assume that there is
no friction from air. You assume that the force of gravity is constant and so on. So when you model this
system you assume the ball to be a particle and so on. Okay. So the model that we build is a wrong model
but the model turns out to be useful because the answer that you get from such a model has error but the
error is minuscule with that minuscule error you can get many useful things done okay and same thing is
true about the artificial neural networks so current model that we have is it exact replica of the human
brain it's not at all okay but is it useful it's quite useful okay so But a typical AI model would have a large
number of neurons as processing units. So if if I have to draw it quickly for you I have to visualize this. Okay.
So I can visualize these neurons as the processing units and there are billions of them and they are arranged
in various layers. So layer after layer. So this is one particular layer followed by another layer and there are
these multiple units which are there. A typical AI models that we look at today would have hundreds of such
layers and these neurons are connected to the neurons in the next layers and the neurons in the next
layers. Okay. And you would have large number of connections between them. And typically your data
would flow from the initial layer to the next layer to the next layer and finally the output. That's the the very
simple view of internals of the AI models. So number of such units we have is about 10^ 9 and number of
such connections we have is about 10^ 12. Okay. uh and these connections they play a crucial role in
building our networks because each of these connection has a weight which tells us what is the importance
of each of the connection. Okay. So whenever this neuron it receives input from all the previous neurons
out of these which inputs are important and which inputs are not important. Okay, that is decided by the
weight of the connection, the importance of the connection. And whenever we talk about training the AI
models, you might have heard this thing, right? We want to train the model. We want to we want model to

learn. We're talking about understanding the importance of these connections. Okay, so training the AI
models is all about understanding which connections are important and which connections are not
important. Okay, that's all that AI training is about. Okay. So let's get back. So if if you want to understand
the internals of AI model, there are neurons, uh there are connections, there are layers and all the
connections have different weights indicating the importance of those connections. Okay. And now what
we have to do is we have trained this AI system. So when you start with a new model, when you say that I'm
going to build a new AI model for certain thing, you have no clue about which connections are important,
which connections are not important. And it's not one or two connections that we are talking about. We are
talking about trillions of connections because there are about 10^ 12 connections that we are talking about.
There are 10^ 9 neurons and each neuron is connected to 10^ 3 other neurons. You multiply them, you get
10^ 12, which is about a trillion. So think about this system. We have a system where you have to guess the
value of 10^ 12 different variables. And how are you going to do it? Okay. Uh think about solving a simple
simultaneous equation that you might have done your high school maths, right? If 2x + y is equal to 7 and 3x
+ 7 y= 9, what is the value of x? What is the value of y? That's the kind of simultaneous equations you might
have dealt with. Now think about a system that has not two variables but a system that has one trillion
variables. Okay? X1, X2, X3, X4 up to X trillion. A system that has one trillion variables and you don't know
the value of any of those variables and you have to guess the value of all those trillion variables. Does it
sound like like an easy problem to do? Does it sound like a problem that you would like to solve? Well, if
you're ambitious enough, you would like to solve. Okay? And you might know a very simple thumb rule
from your basic mathematics. If you want to solve a system of two variables, how many equations you
require? You require two equations. If I just say that 2x + y is equal to 5 and what is the value of x and what
is the value of y? You cannot give one concrete answer. There are infinite possible answers. But the
moment I give you two equations in two variables, you can give me one specific solution. So if I have to find
out the answer for a system of one trillion variable, how many equations I would need? I would need trillion
equations. Okay, which means that I would require a trillion data points as well. And typically you won't find
anywhere data that is that big enough and that is available to you and even if that was available the
processing might not be realistic. So typically the number of data points that are available to you are far less
than the number of parameters that you have. So now we are in a tricky situation. We want to guess the
value of 1 trillion variables but the number of equations we have is going to be far less than that. Which
means that are we going to have one specific solution? We cannot. Okay. And that's why if you train your
model multiple times, you might end up with multiple different values of these variables. And that's why
you might have models that are different if you train multiple times. Okay. Uh again because we are uh not
going into technical details, I I would avoid going into details of how we ex exactly get these values. But
typically what you do is you take one data point you give it as input to your uh network. So if you have data
point let's say D1 you pass it through your network and it will generate output let's say 01. While doing that
it will interact with every neuron in your network. It will also interact with every connection in your
network. And based on this interaction, you can create one particular equation that tells you that if I give
this input, I receive this output. With one data point, you can create one equation. Fine. And with each data
point, you will create a new equation. And you will have billions of such equations. If your data has billions
of data points and now you are trying to solve this system of linear equation uh not linear but system of
simultaneous equations. Okay? uh and while doing that what you want is you want that the model should
faithfully predict the output if input is D1 and output is supposed to be 01 initially output will not be 01
because you don't know the right values of the connections so your output will be wildly different and that's
how you measure the error what was the expected output and what is the output that my system is
generating and what you want is whenever you train your system over a long period of time the error
should reduce. Okay. Uh and there are mathematical methods available to do that. Okay. That's how you
train your data. And now you understand the importance of data. You might have heard a lot about oh data

5 minutes. There is no guarantee that you will get same output tomorrow. So for the same input, what
output you get? uh there is no guarantee okay and that's the crucial difference between the previous
revolutions that we had versus the AI revolution that we have the machines the engines that we built 200

And Abhishek Singh, DG NIC and Additional Secretary Mahati join us to talk more about the India AI Impact
Summit to be held between February 16th and 20th. Sir, first of all I would like to know from you that this
conference is just beginning. However, even before that, we are seeing the kind of praise that has come
from various countries of the world and the United Nations, how do you feel about it and how prepared
does India seem to be to take the lead in this entire region with this platform? See, under the guidance of
the Honorable Prime Minister, India has made its indelible mark in the field of Digital India , in the field of
technology, in the field of AI for a long time. Our main strength is our talent. Engineers from our country go
to all countries and work there. Provides leadership to all the companies. The vision given by the Prime
Minister at the French Action Summit on AI for Humanity is how we can ensure inclusion. How can we
create AI that can provide more benefits to people? So he is guiding the entire AI discussion that is
happening globally today. The report that was talked about is the Scientific Panel Report of Yova Benzi and
what the Secretary General of the UN said, it reflects India's view in the same direction that how on one
hand we can use AI to solve global problems related to health, education, agriculture and meet the needs of
common people and at the same time we can create such AI which is safe so that it does not cause any
harm to individuals, we can label the AI â€‹â€‹generated content so that common people who deal with AI
can understand whether they are watching AI or something else. So this balance between innovation and
safety is India's unique approach which is appreciated by the entire world and we will showcase the same to
the entire world in this AI Summit. Abhishek Singh ji, before this platform of AI, how do you see that this
platform is ready for the entire world and especially the way we are talking about balancing because once
again the situation regarding this technology, Artificial Intelligence, should not become such in the future or
even in the current times that developed countries are assured of access to everything but developing
countries and those countries of Global South remain deprived of it, so how do you see this platform, how
are you seeing it in terms of participation ? Look, this summit which is being held in India from 16th to 20th
February, this is the first time such a summit is being held in the Global South. This is happening in a
developing country. Previous summits were held in Blessley Park in the UK in 2023, in Korea in 2024 and in
France last year. So, another expectation from all the developing countries in India and the Global South is
that how can they also become partners in this era of technology and AI, not only become users of AI but
their voice should also be heard in the development, deployment and governance of AI. So India has taken
a lot of leadership in digital technology. Earlier also, the name Digital Public Infrastructure was coined
during the G20 Summit under India's presidency. So, all the countries of the world, especially the
developing countries and the countries of the Global South, want to know from India how we can
democratize AI. How can we ensure that AI is not determined only by selected countries and selected
companies, but that every country, every country's scientists, every country's researchers, every country's
startups, they too get an opportunity to join this era of AI, they get access to compute, GPUs, data sets,
algorithms, applications, then India is succeeding in putting the agenda of democratizing AI for the Global
South at the forefront in this summit for the first time in many years. Yes Abhishek Singh ji, we are seeing
that big provisions have been made for AI in the budget as well, hence this platform is for the future of India
i.e. for developed India 2047 when India is moving forward. AI is going to be a very important area which
the United Nations President has also clearly mentioned that in the coming times, AI is going to play a big
role in global relations also. So how do you see these relations improving through this platform and what
kind of impact do you see at the global level after this platform which will also help India in its development
journey. Look at the decisions taken in the budget regarding the data centres that will be built in India and
the data centres that will be built by global countries. In that, we are giving them tax extension till 2047.
They are giving them benefits in Safe Harbor. So its main objective is to make India a data center capital of
the world, AI inferencing capital of the world. Electricity is available in abundance in our country. We are
among the leading countries in the world in renewable energy. The cost of building a data center is almost

half in our country. When we compare with countries like America or even the Middle East, data centres
are ready in our country in a very quick time. So, AI computing is required for the inferencing of AI
applications of various countries. So, influencing is a service that India can provide and because our country
already has talent, this talent and when the capability of data center infrastructure becomes available, then
we will be able to create AI use cases, AI applications, global competency centers and all these things and
many countries and many companies are willing to invest in India, which will not only create employment in
our country and bring economic growth in our country, but by providing all these services to the whole
world, our relations will also improve. Yes Abhishek Singh ji, you have mentioned that in the coming times,
employment in the field of AI will also increase. So we would like you to also tell us which new employment
areas will open up for the youth in the field of AI or how should they pursue their studies in the next 10 to
15 years so that new opportunities open up for the youth. See, many new jobs will open up in the AI
â€‹â€‹ecosystem and data ecosystem, in which people who have proficiency in AI will get good
opportunities. They will also get good salary. They will also get good opportunities. The whole world is
seeing how there is a huge need for AI transformation engineers , forward deployment engineers, those
with expertise in agentic AI , data scientists, and data analysts. So for this every person, no matter what

was basically manual jobs which got impacted a lot more. Um that being the case um you know given that
the total number of white collar jobs in the global south by and large and in India specifically are
significantly lower than uh in other parts of the world. Um you know the the immediate impact in that

sense is likely to be a little more muted in the aggregate. But having said that, I think it's also important to
recognize two things. one that uh um for a country like India which has a fairly large ITI it sector we'll really
need to pivot and we'll have to figure out a way by which we can uh uh see that our IT workforce is able to
uh uh adapt to the new reality and uh instead of tasks which they performed previously of coding and
programming and so on try to see whether they can build more applications and deploy more applications.
So those could be the opportunities. So there could be newer job opportunities is the way we read it. Uh
this is what industry also tells us in our interactions with them and which then means that reskilling and
upskilling substantial portion of that workforce is very important. So that becomes an important um area
that we approach. The second possibility is um uh you know I mean we we assume that the delivery of
services and all of those deliverable levels will stay where we are today and therefore uh you can do that
with far fewer resources. The other way to look at it is that you can deliver um far superior services to what
you're delivering now and more services than what you're delivering now. and that provides you the
opportunity to actually absorb uh more people into the into the workforce and I think that is another
possibility which needs to be certain as well. So this is the direction in which we would like from a policy
perspective to take it. Yes, please. Yes, very much so. We are awaiting we have some tentative
confirmations or tentative word from them but uh we are awaiting final confirmation. Google of course I
already mentioned Google deep mind CEOs expected to be present but uh yes even others come. Yeah,
thank you.

Chief Minister of Mealesh. We congratulate Sama Gi, officials of the Ministry of Electronics and IT in the
government of India. Officials of the government of Megal, Assam, industry leaders, founders,
academicians, researchers and my dear young friends from the various universities and colleges who are
joining this uh summit. We are going to organize the AI impact summit in the coming February 2026. As all
of us are aware, our prime minister Shindarendra Modi GI has given us the vision of democratizing
technology. As a part of that mission, as a part of that big focus, we are organizing the regional AI impact
conferences throughout the country. And in this conference at Shalom, the focus will be on people, planet
and progress, which are the three pillars, the three sutras of the AI impact summit. The entire world, the
world of work, the world of entertainment, the world of computing, everything is changing with the
development of AI. How do we make it work for India? How do we make it work for people? How do we
make it useful for our society? That's the mission which our government is working on. As a part of this

mission, we have allocated 10,300 cr rupees over 5 years. We have created a common compute facility so
that everybody can get access to AI resources and more than 38,000 GPUs have so far onboarded at a
fraction of the cost of the global cost. These these common computes GPUs are now available to everybody
for developing applications for using and for creating new knowledge. We have created about 3,800 data
sets which can be used for training models and more than 250 models are today available. Opensource
models which can be used which can be basically converted into many applications. Our target is to train
about 1 million people in AI skills because that is going to define the employability in future. Um there are
many teams and startups which are working on developing good highquality indigenous foundational
models. 12 such teams have shown very good progress so far. Overall the focus is to make sure that every
part of the country gets access to this new technology. Many uh people today say that the way electricity is
an essential part of our modernday living. Same way AI will become an essential part of our modern day
living. So it's very important that AI reaches each and every person who can make use of it for increasing
productivity and for solving large problems. So it is in this approach that we are setting up 570 data and AI
labs. 30 labs have already been launched. One lab is already functioning set up in NET Shilong and two more
labs will be set up at ITI Shillong and Shilong poly techni. Uh another lab is in pipeline at ITI TRA. So this is
the uh overencompassing uh thought process and execution that we are doing. I thank honorable chief
minister Shri Conrad Sana Gi for your patronage of this uh function and I hope that through this conference
many new ideas will come which can be a part of the AI impact summit in faculty hall. Thank you very much
and wish you all the best. Heat. Heat.

SPEAKER / SESSION: S. Krishnan, Secretary, Ministry of Electronics & IT, Government of India, shares key

Sri Warma of ET government my colleagues in the government Dr. Rajendra Kumar the secretary of border
management, Shrimati Nidhi Kare the secretary in the department of consumer affairs and also in the
ministry of new and renewable energy sources and Shri Arvind Kumar the director general of software
technology parks of India Limited. Uh I'm delighted to be present with you here this morning. I think this is
an extremely important event dealing with a very relevant topic at a very timely moment. India is now of
course well known globally as an information technology power and also in addition known as a country
which has made information technology work for the people through digital public infrastructure and the
uniqueness of India's model of digital public infrastructure is the way that public entities have come
together to create platforms forms to create a mechanism upon which private enterprise can build and
deliver services to the people and that has been truly the unique feature of DPI which has been created in
India. India believes that the public infrastructure itself requires to be publicly owned on a notfor-profit
basis in opensource in in a sort of radically open manner on which businesses can build and thereafter
grow. And this is a model which is now celebrated across the world. It has found recognition amongst many
peer countries across the world. In the last G20 summit which was hosted in India in 2023, DPI became an
important element of the way India's own image was projected and that has continued to be an important
theme for the subsequent G20s hosted by Brazil and now in South Africa and globally practically every
country that one visits or every place that one goes the first question which is asked about is how prevalent
ent digital public infrastructure is in India and the way in which it has come to actually serve the people of
this country significantly better. So adoption of many of these elements of digital public infrastructure is
what makes it truly meaningful and truly gamechanging. The more more people who adopt it, the more
transactions which take place, the more ways in which this becomes important is what makes a true

difference to the economy and what gives it momentum. And this is something which is not easy to
preserve because there are many many reasons why people would want that these issues it should not be
as prevalent because you know commercially it is it does harm certain interests. If it is an open-source open
standards kind of mechanism, there are other issues which come up with it. The way in which services are
delivered are much quicker. Um many other economic opportunities or well quasi economic opportunities
that people have may go away with this but the manner in which the services are delivered the speed with
which they are delivered can truly accelerate economic growth and ensure that you know the benefits
reach the people whom it needs to reach and I think that's been the significant element of what has
happened so far. Of course at the bottom of it all is the identity layer. Aadhaar today is available with 1.4
billion citizens and residents of India and people are able to use it for authentication purposes. I mean as
people who have worked in government the importance of having a manner in which identity can be
authenticated is extremely significant. Many people suffer from the want of it and we can well understand
why this is important and what it ultimately enables. It enables anybody to be a participant in the broader
economy in a very seamless and quick fashion. The the friction elements of it, the frictions of transactions
are completely removed. On top of that, when you add something like UPI, which is a payment interface
and when it becomes an Aadhaar enabled payment system, then it simplifies matters so much in terms of
the way the transactions are done and the way that payments are made. When the when our host this uh
hostess this morning introduced and said she found that you know street side vendors and hawkers and
others are using Aadhaar enabled payments and mobile enabled payments. What is happening is something
which is truly profound because what is happening is there's a record of transactions which is getting built
and based on the record of transactions these people actually become banker. This is the way in which
financial penetration can actually take place at that level. It's a way in which financial penetration can easily
reach all of them. Likewise, if you take the health sector, if you take the skilling sector in terms of the ability
for all of us to store our records, whether they are medical records, whether they are are uh uh educational
degrees or skill degrees, all of them as part of say digilock. Today it is enabled and I I have known through
my career people struggling to get their academic records apostled by various government officials and
entities and today it's easy for the record itself to be verified on an API basis from any university across the
world saying that this is the academic records which relate to this person. It makes life so much easier. It
makes things so much faster and it removes so much of friction costs in the economy that we we often do
not measure this and we do not realize how much of a benefit it has actually delivered. On top of all this
eventually when we are able to and I can go on there are 16 kinds of DPIs that we have launched in the
country ways in which these DPIs have been used and um they have been added on and layers have been
built. Sometimes it's amazing to believe that you know we don't even realize how extensive their use has
been and how many people are actually using it on a daily basis. the billions of financial transactions that
take place, the billions of the millions and millions of digilocker accounts that have been o opened, the way
in which academic records are being stored and shared. All of this represents a revolution in each of those
segments of um service delivery and is starting to make a significant difference to all of this and it is
something which is becoming worth imitating across the world. We very frequently receive delegations
almost about half a dozen to uh more delegations every year who want to study how the DPI mechanism in
India works. We received a very high level delegation from Kenya and South Africa which had about 8 to 10
secretaries equivalent to secretaries to the government of India in their respective countries coming here to
study what exactly is happening. It is arousing that much of curiosity and people want to sort of replicate
this mechanism. We already have 17US with different countries in the world where we can replicate this
where they are interested in taking this message. We are working very closely with the ministry of external
affairs to make sure that we are in a position to share our expertise with countries across the world. And
what is most remarkable in this is that the private sector plays such a big part in every aspect of both the
development of the DPI and eventually the the way it is rolled out and the way that applications are built on
top of those platform layers makes a significant difference. Today if you're talking about OMBC which is one
of the newer DPIs which we are in the process of developing and what difference it can make to small
businesses across the world uh across the country and how they can actually reach out and sell anywhere in
the world that's a significant development which we need to be aware of. So in this sense every aspect of
human life, every aspect of life in India is going to be increasingly touched by these DPIs and the power of
this and the whole range of data that they collect can truly be leveraged in the age of artificial intelligence
as well because ultimately artificial intelligence requires data to make it more uh um more accurate and

SPEAKER / SESSION: Shri S. Krishnan, Secretary MeitY ｜ Message on India’s DPDP Act & Digital Trust ｜
Data Safeguard [kujVEGX6mas].en
====================================
Good morning to all of you. Let me first apologize for not being able to be present in person at this
significant event which is taking place today. India today stands at a defining inflection point in its digital
journey. We have built population scale digital infrastructure including Aadhaar, DPI, Digilocker, Coven etc.
which are grounded in both scale and trust. This is what makes digital personal data protection so
important. The act is now operational. We are entering the next phase of this journey. One that places the
citizen firmly at the center of the digital economy. The DPDP act seeks to balance individual rights and
dignity, innovation and the ease of doing business. The act and rules were not rushed. There was rigor at
every stage. We consulted widely and we spoke to a large number of people. The draft rules alone received
over 6,000 comments reflecting deep engagement with stakeholders. There were multiple rounds of
stakehold uh consultations with a range of stakeholders and with a range of people who were concerned
with this. This level of participation has materially shaped the final framework. At its core, the DPDP act is
anchored in the principle that trust is the most critical element. Importantly, the DPDP act reflects an Indian
approach to data protection. It is distinct from other places. I'm glad that today uh the technology based
tools which have been created are in fitting with what the act requires. The act itself is simple, principlebased and technology agnostic. It is tailored to India's soio, economic and institutional realities. It is aligned
with the global best practices while remaining made for India. The act is what we call sur that is simple,
accessible, rational and actionable law. It uses plain language, contains illustrations that make the meanings
clear, contains no provisos, and has minimal cross referencing by using the word she instead of he for the
first time. It acknowledges women in parliamentary lawmaking. The act provides for certain rights to
individuals and these include amongst others the right to access information about personal data processed
and uh the right to correction and erasure of data the right to forget as it right to be forgotten as it's called
the right to grievance redress cell and the right to nominate a person to exercise air rights in case of death
or incapacity this is uniquely Indian in scope and the way that it has been done. The key point here I think
which we need to remember is the act is also very timely given the requirements of data for artificial
intelligence. And I think what it does create is the fact that we have today a system whereby most of the
regulation is technical and at the same time it gives rights to the data principle as to how their data should
be used and becoming a completely digital law. It is possible for us to actually enforce it in a
technologydriven work. Compliance under the DPDP act should be viewed as a one-time or box ticking
exercise. It requires a shift in organizational mindset. We expect organizations both public and private to
move towards privacy by design and privacy by default. Institutionalizing grievance redress and
accountability mechanisms. Embedding privacy into business processes and not treating it as an
afterthought. These are crucial points. Likewise, technology will be a crucial enabler here as I already
pointed out particularly automation. AI assisted governance and interoperable compliance platforms that
reduce cost and complexity are crucial. Implementation readiness is also extremely important. This is the
implementation of the act is a shared responsibility between the government, industry and ecosystem
partners. From the government's side, focus areas include operationalization of the digital data protection
board of India, clear consultative rulemaking, capacity building across ministries, PSUs and state
governments. For industry and enterprises, especially large organizations and PSUs, board level ownership
of data protection, investment in people, processes and technology, and sector specific adoption models
reflecting operational realities are very important. We also recognize the need to support MSMES and
startups with scalable affordable compliance solutions ensuring that the regulation does not become a
barrier to innovation. Indigenous technology is again very key. A key pillar of India's digital strategy is the
development of sovereign trusted indigenous technology capabilities. This is the way we build strategic
autonomy. Homegrown privacy technologies including automation and AIdriven governance tools can lower
compliance costs, improve consistency and auditability and strengthen national digital resilience. This aligns
with our broader vision of building a globally respected digital product from India and and not just for India
but for the whole world. In the next steps that I would like to outline, the effective implementation of the
DPDP act will enhance citizen confidence in digital services, enable responsible datadriven innovation,
strengthen India's position as a trusted digital economy. At the same time, privacy, data protection, and
innovation are not competing objectives. When designed well, they reinforce each other. India has the
opportunity to build trustled growth particularly in an environment where artificial intelligence is becoming

so important and the kind of tools that we create to enable this including the tool that is being launched
today I think is an important step forward in this journey. Thank you and

actors. and I am joined by a truly one-off panel to kick the tires of this question. We're here to look into four
key questions. Is it desirable and indeed possible to reach international consensus on red lines for AI? What
might the vehicles be to drive this consensus? What are the options for feasible and proportional
enforcement? And what are the actual practical ways forward given the geopolitical context that we're in?

Humane Intelligence. Thank you. Thank you. Um there we go. We're on. I will I will also project a little bit
with my voice. Um so one thing I'm very excited about just to start framing the conversation around red
lines and what red lines means is first we have to have an approach of how we want what is the mechanism
by which we arrive at red lines. And there's really two models for us to think about and I think there's
something for this panel to digest as well and maybe for us to dig into. So the first is a sort of traditional
model of uh risk assessment and risk evaluation that is more common to the corporate model and and in
this world what you do and this a lot of the work I do um with with enterprise companies is we help
companies map their risk appetite. we help we help them understand the risks being introduced by AI
models and then you sort of map said appetite to said risks and say okay there's this threshold after this

threshold we will not be using the system or we have you know sort of mechanisms that come into play so
that's one model often the red lines conversation though is really about extreme risk so extreme harm
things like manipulation or runaway AI systems or or bionucle nuclear and biological weapons and actually
in that case there is sometimes a different model and and it's you know sort of known as like the vaccine
model. So just in this scenario you could have a disease that kills five out of 100 people. You and can go in a
lab a scientist can go in a lab and let's say create a vaccine for that disease that um only has a mortality rate
of one in a 100 people. But actually what happens is that vaccine will never be mass- prodduced because
there is a hard red line to say that this vaccine actually needs to be 100% safe before we are able to release
it. Even though we know that there is an additional four deaths due to the existence of this disease and you
know in in expectation you would go down to only one person. So those are two very very different
approaches to drawing red lines. Why is that important here? because that changes the mechanism by
which actually frankly most companies work in identifying risk and also changes the mechanism by which
we even think about uh test and evaluation which is specifically what I work on. How does that change?
Well, first then the kind of testing and evaluating you're doing is not actually holistic testing to generally see
what the impact of the model will be. Then you do very traditional red teaming which is pushing the model
to its most extreme circumstance. So for example, if you are interested in CBRN, biological, radiological,
nuclear weapons, you don't worry about what is the probability that somebody might be able to get some
ingredients. What you do is try to make one instance of it happen. And if you're able to create one instance,
then you say, well, then this model cannot move forward. And again, I think because we have not had
clarity on which mechanism of red lines we want to draw, we cannot then back into clarity on how we can
even have multilateral agreement on testing or enforcement. &gt;&gt; Fantastic. Thank you. We're now

&gt;&gt; Thank you so much. And we now have our last provocation uh from Mariel Muala who's the colead of digital and tech policy selection at the Federal Department of Foreign Affairs of Switzerland. Thank
you. &gt;&gt; Thank you very much. So I'll try and sum up what my uh predecessor said. [laughter] &gt;&gt;
[gasps] &gt;&gt; Um, thank you so much. It's an honor to be part of this discussion. Um, many of you will
rightly emphasize the importance of human rights, fairness, and inclusive participation in AI governance.
From the Swiss side perspect from the Swiss perspective, I'd like to focus on how we turn broad principles
into practical enforcable action and what Switzerland can contribute, especially in the leadup to the AI
summit in Geneva in 27. AI poses risks throughout its development and supply chain from data collection
and model training to deployment in areas such as health, finance, and public services. These include
threats to privacy and non-discrimination, safety, and reliability concerns and the risk that governance
remains fragmented across borders. Addressing these risks requires clear red lines, but red lines that are
vague or hard to enforce. To be meaningful, they must be precise, measurable, and grounded in shared
technical standards. This is not just a matter of good intentions. It determines whether we can monitor,
audit, and hold actors accountable. Because AI systems span multiple countries and jurisdictions,
multilateral cooperation is essential and Switzerland has long supported international collaboration on
technology governance whether through the OECD, the Council of Europe's AI convention, which we see as
a success or processes at the UN. We see these efforts as building the foundations for interoperable
approaches that reduce fragmentation and support consistency. Looking ahead to the AI summit in Geneva
in 27, Switzerland aims to help shape a practical multilateral agenda that bridges technical expertise,
regulatory experience, and diverse geopolitical perspectives. Geneva is home to key actors and we are
committed to using that ecosystem to advance shared definitions, monitoring tools and capacity building
mechanisms. We also recognize geopolitical realities. A single binding global treaty on every aspect on AI
may be unrealistic and in the short term. That's why Switzerland supports incremental progress,
coordinated standards, shared incidents reporting, mutual recognition of compliance regimes, and
collaboration on public procurement strategies that reinforce red lines through incentives as well as rules.
In these efforts, Switzerland offers neutrality, convening capacity, and an inclusive platform for dialogue.
Our goal for 27 and beyond is to help achieve consensus on what matters most and to translate that
consensus into governance frameworks that work in practice. &gt;&gt; Please do join me in thanking our
speakers for their opening provocations because I thought it was a really wide ranging [applause] perfect
introduction to the concept of red lines and how we take it I think from the slightly rarified boardrooms that
these conversations on red lines are often had into practical enforcement. Um we are now going to have a a
bit of a conversation for 10-15 minutes with the panel and then I will be bringing the rest of the room in. So
please do start gearing up with your questions. Roman, you and I have been in many rooms recently where
red lines are very differently discussed and I wondered if you could give the audience a bit of a feel for what
you're hearing when people talk about red lines and maybe the delta between what we've heard so far and

Doctor Paul Distinguished Panelist Esteem Guest Presenting Very Divergent Take Holders Dear Friends,
colleagues, it's an honour to be here S V R S V Heard Today in the Morning And also from Doctor Paul AI is
very Ripidly transitioning from an Experimental tool to a core enabler of Modern Health Systems and Global

Momentum Around AI in Health Is Accelerating For For multitudes of reasons but one we saw Just Heard
Was Its Sheer Potential For Transfative Impact and We Saw All the Dimensions in which AI can be
Contributing I mean obviously around Diagnostics and Clinical Decisions Support Systems Precision
Medicine Public Health Surveillance Health Systems Management Biomedical Research Esetra Esetra But
Unlikely Earlier Digital Health Tools Too With which we have become very familiar AI brings in autonomy it
brings in Learning and What It Brings In Prediction. Now That Raises Both Unprecedented Opportunities But
Also Unprecedented Challenges and Complexities Governance Ethical and Safety Challenges. So ah what we
need to understand is that AI in healthcare should not be viewed as a Technology deployment but more as
a health System intervention. Now my conversation I mean now I mean you No previously about five 10
years back To be discussion around weather to have AI and Not to Have AI But Today's Discussion Is Not
About Ah AI or Not Weather We Like It Or Not as there to stay. So basically we need To Now Focus On How
To Deploy It Responsibly. And my talk ends The presentation here will be focusing on where The World
Stands on AI and Health and Where India is uniquely positioned to leave So What You're Seeing Here Is the
Global North Countries You Know September 2025 September 25 November 25 December In the Last Four
Five Months Global Global North Countries Are Moving Very Rapidly and Disruptively Towards the formal
sector-specific AI Strategies for Health So Earlier We Had Overall AI Strategy But Now People Realize The
Complexity of the Health Sector and Moving on for AI strategies for health. So the USAI strategy for help
was lotch In September 2025 and structured around Five Pillars Empowering Safety Equity and innovation
also have the UK's national Commission to regulate AI in health Care It Reflex Regulatory First The Latest
Approach to AI in Health Care WHO Euro did a study on European Countries and Found That Only Four
Another 50 countries have a national AI strategy For health, but I'm sure that's likely too Change Soon The
Fans Strategy For AI National AI Strategy for Health It Treats Health Data and AI as Strategic National Assets
Now you can So All the Strategies One Thing They Are Recognizing Is Health Care as a very high impact but
high Respect high high risk AI domain so you Will Have Both High Impact But Also High Risk domain and
policy focus is on Shifting from experimentation to Scale Regulation Transparency and Accountability and All
Strategies SV Heard the CO NHA R Link to National Digital Health and Data Governance Framework Sorry So
now for India's journey from AI Journalist to AI so India began with a Broad Sector Agnostic Approach We
Had The 2018 We Launch the AI â€‹â€‹Portal Which Position India's the First Digital First Innovation India
Emerges as a Nation Oriented Nation by 2022 Among the Top 10 Countries with a National AI Strategy Now
This Strategy Focused on Cross Sectoral Capability Such as Data Skills and Innovation however sector
agnostic strategies have Limitations for high-risk domains like Health Care and the RBI Move Vi Sector
Specific AI Frameworks and That has provided a pivotal policy shift and This signals recognition of sectoral
Complexity and more sector specific Strategies Now Health Care Too Requires Tailored AI Governance Due
to Patient Safety and Ethical Concerns and Privacy This is why India is now transitioning from A
comprehensive sector agnostic Horizontal AI policy to a vertical one Sector Focus Strategy Such as the
Strategy For AI in Healthcare for India and We Are having a series The CEO The Ministry Leading with the
Series of Consultations Across The Country and Its Transition Positions India to become a global leader
among the Global South Countries and We Could Possibly Have the launch of a strategy for AI in 2026 Now
when you look at India's journey from National Health Policy To AI Strategy for Health Care. So 2017 We

and Family Welfare And UNDP Realizes That We Already Build A Robust Framework to Covin Can We
Repurpose it to then deliver vaccinations for Newborn Babies and Pregnant Countries And that's where
using the Covin Architecture We Were Able to Build and Deliver UN which delivers vaccine at the Scale of 80
million beneficiaries of India. Now Moving to AI One of the Biggest The Problem That You Have Been
Looking to Solve Is That When a New Born Baby Is Born And when a pregnant mother is admitted for
Delivery There Is A Lot Of Manual Work That What the Nurses Need to Do to Start Recording Their pre-birth
pre-birth data. And That's Where the First Use Case I Am Presenting the AI â€‹â€‹use case is of a
Registration of pregnant women and children Here Rather Than Nurses Using Laptops Too Continuously Do
Clerical Jobs We Said We'd Make Them Focus on Childbirth and Take on the Problem Away and Solve It

Piloted at two hospitals Ready to be rolled out for the entire Country. What does the AI â€‹â€‹solution do?
If You Look at this this is the registration details For a pregnant mother. You Need to Capture Her menstrual
period, blood type, date of Last pregnancy and so on and so fourth. Now What We Said Is Can a Nurse Just
Use An application and talk in it. And It Can Be in any language. It [Music] Can Be In English, Hindi, Marathi,
any mutual Language. And as they do that I put in a Use Case Real Use Case Hear the Nurse Speaks On to
the application the application starts Putting the Data Age Is in the Language That the The nurse is speaking
in and then at the back end We Have a Large Language Model What We Call an LLM which is able to
interpret All of This Conversation That the Nurses Had with the application and then auto populate this
Field for Pregnant Women Registration and child birth registration the viz for Ministry of Health was to
achieve 75% Creation of health records at the time of Bother But We Were Only at 25% and with this AI
Solutions We Will Soon Be At A Stage Where You Will Be Able to Create the First electronic health record for
a Newborn child using AI and this Links very well to the ABDM because if You're looking at Longitudinal
Health Records for the Citizens of Country It Starts with the first CHR which is Your Vaccine Details Which

solution that is currently being built. You're Solving This Problem Using AI There is a patient his name is
Gaurav this is Gaurav's Longitudinal Health Journey He Has Had Three Encounters So Far and Now What He
what he wants to do is he wants to upload it Latest medical documents into the Application so you can drag
and drop a choose A File Gaurav Selects H Medical Record which is from a pathology department It's the
PDF and it has got Unstructured data And now what happens is you uploaded the Document Report. Now
When We Move To The Next Step We Are going to use a generative AI model. there are Many large
language models available. in In this case we use something called NOV Lite Which is an Amazon LLM Large
Language Model. Now This Large Language Model Is Now Extracting all the information from this Medical
records so that it becomes available S text. So you see from that PDF the LLM Has Extracted and It Has
Created an entire list of every single Information that was available in the PDF. So This is the first use of
generative AI that That has happened. What You Need to Do Next Do Is Again Engage a Large Language
Model And LLM to Convert This Entire Information into FHIR. Fast Health Care Interoperable Record. I am
sure this Audience knows what a fire bundle is. so here Again We Engage the Large Language Model and We
have been able to extract the f FHIR Bundle from This Unstructured Data Data set which was on a PDF. Now
Once This Happened You Solve It for Real Problems Using Generative AIs. First Converting Unstructured to
structured and then converting into a Fire Bundle. The Next Step Is ABDM Validation. How do we validate
that this fire? The bundle is actually ABDM compliant. End That's the third use case of generative AI where
When This Fire Bundle Comes In With a Click Of a button you can just say weather it is ABDM Compliance
and Then We Store It Into a data lake. which in this case is Health Lake Which Is a Fire Data Store. So In
three steps you have taken an Unstructured medical data converted into Fire Bundle Looked At Weather It's
ABDM Compliant and uploaded it once for all and This is how you go to the first step Where I started for
this patient Gaurav The Next medical history is also Curated and Generative AI Can Now Start
recommending future interventions for This patient is well. So This Is How We Are Transforming Something
Like an Arogya Setu Inu A 2.0 using generative AI and AI Use Cases. So I have covered to Jane use Cases so
far for national platform. Forest Was for you for birth registry. The Second Is for any PHR patient health

====================================
What is the benefit you see taking this approach of being collaborative rather than being competitive as
